title: Differentiable rendering with perturbed optimizers

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes a new differentiable renderer by introducing perturbed optimizers. The paper re-writes the non-differentiable rasterization and aggregation steps to heaven side functions and relaxes them to be differentiable by adding noise. 
The author shows that previous work, especially softras, can be treated as a special case of the perturbed renderer with specific prior selections. The authors also demonstrated in several experiments they achieve better or comparable results than the state of the art.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths

1) Differentiable reformulation
This paper treats rasterization as a linear programing problem.  It extends the [4] to the rendering pipeline and relaxes the non-differentiable rendering to be differentiable by introducing perturbed optimizers. It provides a new differentiable renderer from a new perspective.

2) Better performance
The authors show AS and VR are very useful in optimization, for both perturbed renderer and softras. In the 6D pose comparison, the results are better than softras.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
There are several weak points of this paper.

1) The paper is not self-contained.  I have a hard time understanding each equation. While the authors largely rely on [4], it makes readers hard to follow without knowing [4]. Moreover, the notations in this paper are very vague.   E.g. line 66 it should be j \in [1,.., m]?  line 76 z should be depth order?  If it is depth, how do you compute <z, y>?  line 88, what's v?  The paper writing definitely needs to be improved.


2) Relation of softras and noise types.  The authors show that softras can be treated as the special case of the perturbed renderer. Softras has some hyperparameters which are hard to explain but in the new renderer, all the parameters have clear noise intensity meaning.  While this is very good, but the paper lacks further analysis of which noise should we use. E.g. in table 1, it seems Gaussian noise is the best. But could it be generalized to other cases? 


3) Comparison.  In line 214 the authors said, "Unless stated, the results for the other renderers are extracted from their original publications, as we were not able to get access to their implementations."  I am a little confused about this.  Clearly, N3mr, softras, dibr released their renderers. Thus, why not simply replace the renderer from perturbed renderer to new renderers and compare the score?  Directly copying scores from others' papers may have the wrong comparison. While all the methods use shapenet, the rendered images could vary from lighting to camera views. I would highly recommend the authors redo the comparison.


4)  The authors assume each pixel will be influenced by all the triangles. For each pixel, it renders the influences of all the triangles and combines them together(Eq 3).  I wonder what's the rendering speed of the perturbed renderer? Theoretically, such design plus the MC sampling will make the renderer very time-consuming.  The authors should show the rendering speed comparison.


5) Following the last concern.  Generally speaking, rasterization accelerates the rendering speed by assuming each pixel is influenced by the frontest triangle.  On the other side, MC is always used in ray tracing to compute multiple bounces. The authors introduce MC in rasterization, which seems to adopt the weak points of two rendering methods.   I wonder did the authors consider extending it to RT?

Post rebuttal:

The authors addressed my concerns and I will raise the score to be marginally above the threshold and be happy to see this paper get accepted.

This paper proposes a new differentiable renderer by introducing perturbed optimizers. The paper re-writes the non-differentiable rasterization and aggregation steps to heaven side functions and relaxes them to be differentiable by adding noise.

Main Review:

reviews.text:
Summary:
The authors present a novel differentiable rendering framework by leveraging perturbed optimizers. They reformulate the traditionally non-differentiable rasterization and aggregation stages as smooth “heaven side” functions and inject noise to make these operations differentiable. The paper further demonstrates that existing approaches, notably SoftRas, emerge as special cases of this perturbed renderer under specific prior choices. Experimental results indicate that their method achieves performance on par with or exceeding the state of the art.

Main Review:

Strengths

1) Differentiable Reformulation  
The paper recasts rasterization as a linear programming problem, extending the ideas from [4] into the rendering pipeline. By introducing perturbed optimizers, the authors turn previously non-differentiable rendering steps into smooth functions. This perspective offers a fresh avenue for building differentiable renderers.

2) Improved Results  
Through experiments on 6D pose estimation, the authors show that both the perturbed renderer and SoftRas benefit from annealed sampling (AS) and variance reduction (VR). Their method outperforms SoftRas on this benchmark, underscoring the practical value of their approach.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Despite its contributions, the paper has several weaknesses:

1) Lack of Self-Containment  
The presentation heavily relies on [4], making it difficult to follow without being familiar with that work. The notation is often ambiguous. For instance, in line 66, should j range over [1,…,m]? In line 76, is z the depth order, and if so, how is the inner product ⟨z, y⟩ computed? What does v denote in line 88? The clarity of the writing needs substantial improvement.

2) Analysis of Noise Choices  
While framing SoftRas as a special perturbed-renderer case is insightful—and assigning clear noise-intensity interpretations to parameters—the paper stops short of advising which noise distribution to pick. Table 1 hints that Gaussian noise works best, but it remains unclear whether this finding generalizes to other tasks or settings.

3) Fairness of Comparisons  
In line 214, the authors note that they borrowed results for competing renderers from their original publications due to unavailable code. However, implementations for N3MR, SoftRas, and DIB-R are publicly released. The authors should have re-run these methods under identical conditions (same lighting, camera parameters, etc.) rather than quoting numbers directly, to ensure a fair apples-to-apples comparison.

4) Runtime Efficiency  
The proposed approach treats every pixel as influenced by all triangles (Eq. 3) and employs Monte Carlo sampling. This design likely incurs a significant computational cost. The paper should include timing benchmarks comparing the perturbed renderer’s speed with other differentiable renderers.

5) Combining Rasterization and Monte Carlo  
Typical rasterization accelerates rendering by considering only the foremost triangle per pixel, whereas Monte Carlo sampling is a cornerstone of ray tracing for handling multiple bounces. By integrating Monte Carlo into rasterization, the method seems to inherit the drawbacks of both techniques. Have the authors considered extending their perturbed-optimizer formulation to a full ray-tracing context?

Post-rebuttal:
The authors have addressed my concerns satisfactorily. In light of their clarifications and additional responses, I raise my evaluation to just above the acceptance threshold and support this paper’s acceptance.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a new differentiable rendering method, differentiable perturbed renderers,  wherein the gradients of the rasterization step are approximated using perturbed optimizers. They implement this change on top of the SoftRas method and demonstrate that it leads to better pose estimation, and on par performance with state of the art methods in unsupervised single image 3d object reconstruction.
reviews.main_review:
This is a strong paper. It is written clearly, and the method is explained properly. Perturbed optimizers seem to be a new way for tackling optimization over discrete operations in deep learning, and their application to rasterization is well motivated and appropriate. The theory required for applying them to this new setting seems right, and is well explained. The results are also adequate for the claims made. It would have been nice to see higher performance on the reconstruction task, though diff rendering is a relatively new and so highlighting new avenues to explore , such as your proposed perturbed optimizers,  is important, and so I do not believe it is necessary.

 I would say one nice set of experiments to see would be optimization over the same set of parameters as in the DIB-R  paper Figure 2. ( camera position, texture , vertex positions, etc. ), with qualitative and quantitative comparisons to DIB-R and SoftRas. Having this would go a long was to increasing my score, and I believe would sell you paper quite a bit better. The pose estimation seems quite a bit easier than optimizing for texture and vertex position for example, and it would be nice to see how you method fairs, even if the performance is only on par or worse.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes.

reviews.text:
Summary:  
The authors introduce a novel differentiable rendering technique—dubbed Differentiable Perturbed Renderers—wherein they approximate the gradients of the discrete rasterization process via perturbed optimization methods. Building on the existing SoftRas framework, they show that this modification improves camera pose estimation and achieves competitive results with current state-of-the-art approaches for unsupervised single-image 3D object reconstruction.

Main Review:  
Overall, this is a well-executed and clearly written manuscript. The motivation for leveraging perturbed optimizers to handle the discreteness of rasterization is compelling, and the extension of these optimizers to the rendering context is both theoretically sound and thoroughly articulated. The experimental findings substantiate the claims: the proposed approach yields more accurate pose estimation, and its reconstruction performance is on par with leading methods.

While it would have been impressive to see a larger gain in reconstruction metrics, I recognize that differentiable rendering is still maturing. Highlighting a promising new direction—namely, the integration of perturbed optimizers—is valuable in itself, even if the immediate performance boost is modest.

One set of experiments that could further strengthen the submission would involve optimizing the same parameter set explored in Figure 2 of the DIB-R paper (camera pose, surface texture, vertex coordinates, etc.) and providing both qualitative and quantitative comparisons against DIB-R and SoftRas. Such an evaluation would more directly demonstrate how the perturbed optimizer fares on the full reconstruction pipeline. Even if results merely match or slightly trail those baselines, this comparison would significantly bolster confidence in the method and likely raise the paper’s impact.

Limitations and Societal Impact:  
Addressed by the authors.

Rating:  
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary:
This paper presents a method for converting a standard non-differentiable rasterization-based renderer into a differentiable counterpart using recent advances in the perturbed optimizer. The main contribution lies in the generic nature provided by the theoretical formulation, where some specifically chosen priors in the proposed framework can retrieve recent state-of-the-art differentiable renderers, e.g. SoftRas. 
As the proposed approach mainly relies on a Monte-Carlo estimator for generating the smoothing parameters. Variance-reduction mechanism and sensitivity analysis are also incorporated into the proposed framework to ensure the robustness of the approximate renderer. Experiments on pose estimation and single-view reconstruction are conducted for comparing the performance of the proposed method and previous state-of-the-art approaches.
reviews.main_review:
Pros:

- By introducing the perturbed optimizer to analyzing the non-differentiability of standard rasterization-based rendering pipeline provides new insight into constructing a generic differentiable renderer. The perturbed differentiable rendering can also be implemented on top of existing renderers without major modifications, which makes this approach versatile and a potential performance booster for existing DR techniques.

Cons:

- In terms of technical novelty, it seems to be a direct application of perturbed optimizer to differentiable rendering. Though it requires some efforts (such as the variance reduction and sensitivity analysis) to adapt the existing theory to this specific application, it is not very challenging as there are existing protocols to follow.

- Evaluation-wise, in the task of single-view reconstruction, though the proposed method can achieve comparable performance compared to the state-of-the-art approaches, it still fails to outperform DIB-R in mean accuracy and also outperformed by SoftRas in many categories, such as the Dresser, Car, etc. The 3D pose estimation is only evaluated on a simple cube which cannot provide a very comprehensive measurement of the proposed approach.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

reviews.text:
Summary:
This manuscript introduces a strategy for transforming a classic, non-differentiable rasterization-based renderer into a differentiable version by leveraging recent developments in the perturbed optimizer. The key strength of the work is its broadly applicable theoretical framework: by selecting appropriate priors within this formulation, one can recover existing state-of-the-art differentiable renderers such as SoftRas. The approach hinges on a Monte Carlo estimator to generate smoothing parameters, and it integrates both variance-reduction techniques and sensitivity analysis to bolster the stability of the approximate gradients. The authors validate their method on two tasks—pose estimation and single-view 3D reconstruction—against leading alternatives.

Main Review:

Pros:
- The application of the perturbed optimizer to dissect the non-differentiable components of a standard rasterization pipeline offers a fresh perspective for building a universal differentiable renderer.  
- Since the perturbed differentiable rendering can be layered on top of existing rasterization engines with minimal code changes, it represents a flexible extension that could enhance performance across current differentiable rendering toolkits.

Cons:
- From a novelty standpoint, the core idea essentially amounts to plugging the perturbed optimizer into the rendering context. While adapting the theory (e.g., implementing variance reduction and sensitivity analysis) requires effort, the process largely follows established recipes, which limits the technical challenge.  
- In the single-view reconstruction experiments, although the proposed method matches several state-of-the-art results, it does not surpass DIB-R on overall accuracy and is outperformed by SoftRas on specific categories such as Dresser and Car. Moreover, the 3D pose estimation test is confined to a simple cube scenario, which provides only a limited gauge of the approach’s generalizability.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
The paper acknowledges potential limitations and discusses societal implications.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a differentiable rendering method that builds connection between the 3D model and 2D perspective appearance. Different from previous methods, the differentiable rendering is formulated into an optimization problem. Two computer vision tasks, including pose estimation and 3D shape from color image, are evaluated with the proposed renderer and compared to SoftRas [22].
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
- The performance for pose estimation outperforms existing methods.
- The formulation seems novel.

Weakness:
- Could the proposed formulation be applied to other 3D shape representations, e.g. point cloud, implicit? If so, an experiment should be added. If not, then better to mention this is a mesh-specific technic.

- The algorithm seems to be computational costy, e.g. 1 min per image. Would this heavily affect the training efficiency? While comparing to other methods, e.g. SoftRas, the run-time efficiency and hyper-parameters, e.g. number of iteration, training epoch, should be mentioned. Otherwise the comparison may not be on a fair basis.

- Keeping the boundary differentiable is one of the main issues in existing differentiable rendering technics, which is also argued mainly in this paper. However it is not clear how this is improved by the proposed method. From Fig 2 and 4, it seems the boundary area has obvious artifacts. Also some experiments should be performed to highlight the improvements over occlusion boundary.

- The performance on 3D shape prediction does not outperform existing approaches. 

Overall, despite a novel formulation, the improvements over existing approaches are not clear. As shown in the experiment, the propose method does not necessarily produce more accurate rendering or support better for vision tasks. Considering that the proposed tech is also quite slow, the overall advantage over existing approaches is not very clear.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: No comment.

Summary:
This paper proposes a differentiable rendering method that builds connection between the 3D model and 2D perspective appearance. Different from previous methods, the differentiable rendering is formulated into an optimization problem. Two computer vision tasks, including pose estimation and 3D shape from color image, are evaluated with the proposed renderer and compared to SoftRas [22].

Main Review:
Strength:
- The performance for pose estimation outperforms existing methods.
- The formulation seems novel.

Weakness:
- Could the proposed formulation be applied to other 3D shape representations, e.g. point cloud, implicit? If so, an experiment should be added. If not, then better to mention this is a mesh-specific technic.

- The algorithm seems to be computational costy, e.g. 1 min per image. Would this heavily affect the training efficiency? While comparing to other methods, e.g. SoftRas, the run-time efficiency and hyper-parameters, e.g. number of iteration, training epoch, should be mentioned. Otherwise the comparison may not be on a fair basis.

- Keeping the boundary differentiable is one of the main issues in existing differentiable rendering technics, which is also argued mainly in this paper. However it is not clear how this is improved by the proposed method. From Fig 2 and 4, it seems the boundary area has obvious artifacts. Also some experiments should be performed to highlight the improvements over occlusion boundary.

- The performance on 3D shape prediction does not outperform existing approaches. 

Overall, despite a novel formulation, the improvements over existing approaches are not clear. As shown in the experiment, the propose method does not necessarily produce more accurate rendering or support better for vision tasks. Considering that the proposed tech is also quite slow, the overall advantage over existing approaches is not very clear.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No comment.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
Summary:
This manuscript presents a differentiable renderer that tightly couples a 3D model with its 2D appearance under perspective projection by casting rendering as an optimization problem. The authors evaluate their approach on two vision tasks—pose estimation and single-image 3D shape reconstruction—and compare results against SoftRas [22].

Strengths:
- The method achieves state-of-the-art results on the pose estimation benchmark, surpassing previous techniques.
- The rendering-as-optimization formulation appears to be a novel contribution.

Weaknesses:
- Generality to other 3D representations is unclear. Could this formulation be extended beyond meshes (e.g., to point clouds or implicit surfaces)? If so, an additional experiment should demonstrate it; if not, the manuscript ought to clarify that the technique is mesh-specific.
- The computational cost is high (approximately one minute per image), which raises concerns about training efficiency. To ensure a fair comparison with methods like SoftRas, the authors should report runtime metrics and hyperparameters (e.g., iteration counts, number of training epochs).
- A key challenge in differentiable rendering is maintaining differentiable object boundaries, yet it is not evident how the proposed method addresses this. Figures 2 and 4 show noticeable artifacts along object edges. Targeted experiments focusing on occlusion-boundary handling are needed to substantiate claimed improvements.
- On the 3D shape reconstruction task, the proposed approach does not outperform existing methods, calling into question its practical advantage for this application.

Overall Assessment:
While the optimization-based rendering framework is conceptually interesting, the empirical gains over existing differentiable renderers are ambiguous. The method’s slow speed, lack of clear boundary improvements, and no advantage in shape reconstruction suggest its benefits do not yet justify adoption over established approaches.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
No comments.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough – rejection

- The formulation seems novel.

Weakness:
- Could the proposed formulation be applied to other 3D shape representations, e.g. point cloud, implicit? If so, an experiment should be added. If not, then better to mention this is a mesh-specific technic.

- The algorithm seems to be computational costy, e.g. 1 min per image. Would this heavily affect the training efficiency? While comparing to other methods, e.g. SoftRas, the run-time efficiency and hyper-parameters, e.g. number of iteration, training epoch, should be mentioned. Otherwise the comparison may not be on a fair basis.

- Keeping the boundary differentiable is one of the main issues in existing differentiable rendering technics, which is also argued mainly in this paper. However it is not clear how this is improved by the proposed method. From Fig 2 and 4, it seems the boundary area has obvious artifacts. Also some experiments should be performed to highlight the improvements over occlusion boundary.

- The performance on 3D shape prediction does not outperform existing approaches. 

Overall, despite a novel formulation, the improvements over existing approaches are not clear. As shown in the experiment, the propose method does not necessarily produce more accurate rendering or support better for vision tasks. Considering that the proposed tech is also quite slow, the overall advantage over existing approaches is not very clear.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
No comment.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.original_rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

title: NAS-Bench-x11 and the Power of Learning Curves

reviews.summary:
This paper proposes another benchmark dataset for NAS, NAS-Bench-x11. Compared to the original benchmarks NAS-Bench-101, NAS-Bench-301 and NAS-Bench-NLP, NAS-Bench-x11 contains the full training

reviews.text:
Summary:  
This manuscript introduces NAS-Bench-x11, a new benchmark for neural architecture search (NAS). Unlike prior benchmarks such as NAS-Bench-101, NAS-Bench-301, and NAS-Bench-NLP, NAS-Bench-x11 provides complete training records for every candidate architecture, in particular detailed learning curves. This enables the evaluation of multi-fidelity NAS methods. To scale to the enormous architecture space, the authors build a surrogate model that predicts each learning curve via singular value decomposition coupled with noise modeling.

Strengths:  
1. The motivation is compelling and the exposition is lucid, with clear acknowledgment of the benchmark’s boundaries.  
2. NAS-Bench-x11 will be a useful resource for the NAS community, since it supplies full training histories that simplify analysis and foster the development of new NAS algorithms.  
3. The approach for forecasting learning curves is novel and engaging.  
4. The authors back up their claims with thorough experiments demonstrating the surrogate’s accuracy and the benchmark’s utility.

Weakness:  
• The main novelty is limited, as providing learning curves was already explored in NAS-Bench-201. This overlap diminishes the paper’s originality.

Limitations and Societal Impact:  
Addressed.

reviews.summary: Existing NAS benchmarks often only include the architecture performance at the final or some selected checkpoint epochs. However, per-epoch information is essential for developing and evaluating multi-fidelity NAS algorithms. This work proposed to fit a surrogate model to predict the learning curve given an architecture encoding. Utilizing the proposed predictor, the author extends three existing benchmarks NASBench-101, NASBench-301, and NASBench-NLP to include per-epoch accuracies. Further, to demonstrate the necessity of developing multi-fidelity algorithms for NAS, the author also describes a procedure to extend the single-fidelity algorithms to multi-fidelity ones by using learning curve extrapolation to filter out candidate proposals with partial training. Despite possible room for improvements on the performance of the learning curve predictor used, the proposed method would enable researchers to develop and rapidly test multi-fidelity NAS algorithms. I vote for acceptance.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:
1. The contribution of this paper is relevant to the NAS community and would accelerate research on multi-fidelity NAS algorithms.
2. The performance of the proposed learning curve predictor model is overall satisfactory.
3. The author adopted the proposed method to extend three existing benchmarks to include per-epoch information.

Weakness:
1. The author reported ranking correlation and KL divergence between the predicted learning curve and ground truth. The numbers seem reasonable, but the errors are still non-negligible. It is unclear how misleading these errors would be if someone tries to develop multi-fidelity algorithms by querying the proposed extended benchmark. How would the existing multi-fidelity NAS algorithms perform under the proposed benchmark, over ground-truth data? This might be doable for NASBench201 or NASBench111 (the version created without using information about the given 4 checkpoint epochs), as they provide ground-truth accuracies on at least some checkpoints?

Comments and Questions:
1. For NASBench-NLP, using the accuracy from the first few epochs as features significantly improves the learning curve predictor's performance. Can we also expect visible improvements on other spaces, if the info of the first few epochs is also provided?

2. You mentioned in para 355 that future surrogate works should save full learning curve info to avoid the need for any extra extensions. But surrogate benchmarks like NASBench-301 only train a small portion of the architectures fully, and the large majority part of the search space will be benched by the predictor. So it seems that such surrogate benchmark would still need to be extended with learning curve prediction anyway. Could you elaborate more on this?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

reviews.text:
Summary:
Most existing neural architecture search (NAS) benchmarks report only final or a few selected checkpoint accuracies, yet detailed per-epoch performance is crucial for designing and benchmarking multi-fidelity NAS methods. This paper addresses that gap by training a surrogate model to predict complete learning curves from architecture encodings. Using this predictor, the authors augment three established benchmarks—NASBench-101, NASBench-301, and NASBench-NLP—to provide accuracies at every epoch. They also outline how to turn single-fidelity NAS algorithms into multi-fidelity versions by leveraging partial training and extrapolating learning curves to prune candidate architectures. While there remains scope to improve the predictor’s accuracy, this extension will allow researchers to quickly prototype and evaluate multi-fidelity NAS approaches. I recommend acceptance.

Strengths:
1. The paper makes a timely and relevant contribution to the NAS field by filling an important benchmarking gap, facilitating faster research on multi-fidelity NAS strategies.
2. The proposed learning-curve prediction model achieves overall respectable performance metrics.
3. The authors successfully apply their methodology to extend three widely used NAS benchmarks with per-epoch performance data.

Weakness:
1. Although the reported ranking correlations and KL divergences between predicted and actual learning curves are acceptable, the prediction errors are still noticeable. It remains unclear how these inaccuracies might bias or mislead practitioners who rely on the extended benchmarks for multi-fidelity NAS algorithm development. For instance, how would established multi-fidelity NAS methods fare when evaluated on the surrogate-extended benchmarks versus the true ground-truth per-epoch data? This comparison could be feasible for NASBench-201 or NASBench-111 (the version built without using information from the four provided checkpoint epochs), since they include some ground-truth intermediate accuracies.

Comments and Questions:
1. In the NASBench-NLP experiments, incorporating accuracies from the first few epochs as additional features substantially boosts the predictor’s accuracy. Would a similar approach yield noticeable improvements in the other search spaces if early-epoch data were provided?
2. In paragraph 355, you suggest that future surrogate benchmarks should store full learning-curve data upfront to eliminate the need for post-hoc extensions. However, benchmarks such as NASBench-301 only fully train a small subset of architectures and rely on a predictor for the rest of the space—meaning they would still require learning-curve extrapolation. Could you clarify how your recommendation applies in that context?

Limitations and Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: The authors describe a mechanism to predict learning curves which they use to generate surrogate benchmarks from existing NAS-Bench versions without learning curves. The authors explore different ways to create learning curve embeddings, different ways to predict these embeddings using the architecture representation as an input and different ways to model the training noise. Furthermore, the authors discuss a short study on using learning curve extrapolation in context of speeding-up NAS.
reviews.main_review:
The authors provide a good description of their method, alternative ways to solve each problem faced and support their decisions with empirical evidence.

My main concern is about the usefulness of this surrogate benchmark which has problems as partly discussed by the authors. It remains unclear what impact these problems would have.

The noise modelling in this paper leads to artifacts we would not see in real learning curves. First, it is very unlikely that the noise is architecture- and hyperparameter-independent. Second, given just the very few examples in Figure 1 we observe that the predicted learning curve in early epochs can already exceed to highest value of the entire learning curve (possibly due to very high noise levels).
In general, all surrogate benchmarks have the problem that they introduce some sort of bias which will not match what you would see in reality. Therefore, these datasets are more similar to synthetic datasets than real ones. They might be nice to prototype but I wouldn’t use them to evaluate NAS methods.

The experiment on combining learning curve extrapolation is a nice reminder for the NAS community to keep these approaches in mind when comparing different approaches. However, it provides no novelty since these methods have been used in the context of NAS already (see [1]).
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The authors describe a mechanism to predict learning curves which they use to generate surrogate benchmarks from existing NAS-Bench versions without learning curves. The authors explore different ways to create learning curve embeddings, different ways to predict these embeddings using the architecture representation as an input and different ways to model the training noise. Furthermore, the authors discuss a short study on using learning curve extrapolation in context of speeding-up NAS.

Main Review:
The authors provide a good description of their method, alternative ways to solve each problem faced and support their decisions with empirical evidence.

My main concern is about the usefulness of this surrogate benchmark which has problems as partly discussed by the authors. It remains unclear what impact these problems would have.

The noise modelling in this paper leads to artifacts we would not see in real learning curves. First, it is very unlikely that the noise is architecture- and hyperparameter-independent. Second, given just the very few examples in Figure 1 we observe that the predicted learning curve in early epochs can already exceed to highest value of the entire learning curve (possibly due to very high noise levels).
In general, all surrogate benchmarks have the problem that they introduce some sort of bias which will not match what you would see in reality. Therefore, these datasets are more similar to synthetic datasets than real ones. They might be nice to prototype but I wouldn’t use them to evaluate NAS methods.

The experiment on combining learning curve extrapolation is a nice reminder for the NAS community to keep these approaches in mind when comparing different approaches. However, it provides no novelty since these methods have been used in the context of NAS already (see [1]).

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:  
The paper introduces a framework for forecasting neural network learning curves and uses these predictions to construct surrogate benchmarks from existing NAS-Bench datasets that lack recorded learning curves. The authors investigate several strategies for learning-curve embedding, different prediction models that take architecture representations as input, and multiple approaches to inject training noise. They also present a brief case study on applying learning-curve extrapolation to accelerate NAS.

Detailed Review:  
Strengths:  
• The authors clearly outline their overall approach, detailing alternative solutions for each subproblem and backing up their design choices with experimental results.  

Concerns:  
1. Practical Value of the Surrogate Benchmarks:  
   - Although the paper acknowledges certain shortcomings of the generated benchmarks, it stops short of analyzing their real-world impact. It is unclear how these limitations would affect downstream NAS research.  
2. Noise Modeling Artifacts:  
   - The assumption that noise is independent of architecture and hyperparameters is unrealistic. In practice, different models and tuning settings exhibit distinct noise characteristics.  
   - The few sample curves in Figure 1 already show implausible behavior: some predicted curves in the early epochs surpass the maximum value seen in the true learning curve, likely due to the overly aggressive noise injection.  
3. Bias in Surrogate Datasets:  
   - By design, any surrogate benchmark will distort the true performance landscape. These datasets thus resemble purely synthetic benchmarks more than genuine learning-curve data. While they may serve as handy prototypes, I would hesitate to rely on them for evaluating NAS algorithms.  
4. Novelty of the NAS Speed-up Experiment:  
   - The demonstration that learning-curve extrapolation can speed up NAS is a useful reminder to the community. However, it does not introduce new methodology, since similar extrapolation techniques have already been applied in NAS (see [1]).

Limitations and Societal Impact:  
Yes, the authors discuss limitations and potential broader impacts.

Rating:  
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

Recommendation:  
Reject (marginally below threshold)

reviews.summary: Existing NAS benchmark datasets (e.g., NAS-Bench-101) do not provide full training information of architectures, which make it infeasible to evaluate the multi-fidelity algorithms (e.g., learning curve extrapolation). To address these issues, this paper create surrogate benchmark datasets, namely NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311 and NAS-Bench-NLP11, with singular value decomposition and noise modeling methods. Extensive experimental results with the full training information on these datasets show the superiority of the proposed method.
reviews.main_review:
## Positive points:
1. The paper presents a method to extend the existing single-fidelity benchmarks to multi-fidelity ones with full training information.
2. The authors create NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311, and NAS-Bench-NLP11 benchmark datasets, which may be beneficial to develop new NAS algorithm.

## Negative points:
1. Does the proposed method apply to real world NAS application? In practice, given a dataset, we may have no available training information of any architectures. In this case, how to apply the proposed method? It would be limited if the proposed method is only able to extend the existing NAS benchmarks.
2. In line 220, why do the authors sample architectures from BANANAS, local search and regularized evolution?
3. In line 225, the authors mention that the method is able to improve the performance with the accuracies from the tabular benchmark (at epochs 4, 12, 36, 108) as additional features. More details of this should be provided.

reviews.text:
Summary:
Current neural architecture search (NAS) benchmarks like NAS-Bench-101 lack detailed training trajectories for each architecture, which prevents a fair assessment of multi-fidelity strategies (for instance, learning curve extrapolation). To fill this gap, the authors propose surrogate benchmark suites—NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311, and NAS-Bench-NLP11—constructed via singular value decomposition combined with a noise injection model. Comprehensive experiments leveraging these full training records demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms baselines on these new multi-fidelity benchmarks.

Main Review:
Positive Aspects:
1. The paper introduces a clear procedure to convert existing single-fidelity NAS datasets into multi-fidelity benchmarks by reconstructing full training curves.
2. Delivering three new benchmark collections (NAS-Bench-111, NAS-Bench-311, NAS-Bench-NLP11) offers the community valuable resources for developing and evaluating novel NAS algorithms under multi-fidelity settings.

Constructive Criticisms:
1. Practical Applicability: In many real-world NAS scenarios, one does not have access to any complete training logs for candidate architectures. How would the proposed framework operate when starting from scratch on a new dataset with no precomputed performance trajectories? If it only functions by augmenting existing tabular benchmarks, its utility could be somewhat restricted.
2. Sampling Choices (Line 220): The authors employ architecture samples from BANANAS, local search, and regularized evolution. Please justify why these three search methods were chosen for sampling, and how sensitive the results are to this selection.
3. Feature Augmentation Details (Line 225): The manuscript claims that adding accuracy values at epochs 4, 12, 36, and 108 from the tabular benchmarks boosts performance. Additional explanation or ablation on how these features are integrated, and their quantitative impact, would strengthen the paper.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
The authors have adequately discussed the limitations of their surrogate benchmark generation method and potential negative societal consequences.

Rating:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

Original Decision:
5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Compressed Video Contrastive Learning

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a self-supervised method for compressed video representation learning, which helps save both storage and computation.
reviews.main_review:
Strength:

- The proposed method directly uses RGB and motion vectors in the compressed video for efficient representation learning.
- Experimental results show that the proposed method reduces the total (inference) time and storage, and achieves marginal improvement compared to state-of-the-art methods.

Weakness:

- Adding temporal learning objectives such as changing the temporal order of RGB frames or motion vectors within a clip ( as in IMRNet ), which is missing from the proposed method, would greatly help learning video representation.
- Modeling of the proposed method mostly applies the InfoNCE loss, only selecting of positive and negative samples are updated for RGB and motion vectors.
- As shown in table 1, the computation overhead largely comes from the preprocessing time. Though not an apple-to-apple comparison, parallel re-encoding for IMRNet may largely mitigate the overhead?
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The reviewer would encourage the authors to discuss limitations and impacts of the proposed method, as mentioned in the 'Main Review'.
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper proposes a self-supervised method for compressed video representation learning, which helps save both storage and computation.

Main Review:
Strength:

- The proposed method directly uses RGB and motion vectors in the compressed video for efficient representation learning.
- Experimental results show that the proposed method reduces the total (inference) time and storage, and achieves marginal improvement compared to state-of-the-art methods.

Weakness:

- Adding temporal learning objectives such as changing the temporal order of RGB frames or motion vectors within a clip ( as in IMRNet ), which is missing from the proposed method, would greatly help learning video representation.
- Modeling of the proposed method mostly applies the InfoNCE loss, only selecting of positive and negative samples are updated for RGB and motion vectors.
- As shown in table 1, the computation overhead largely comes from the preprocessing time. Though not an apple-to-apple comparison, parallel re-encoding for IMRNet may largely mitigate the overhead?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The reviewer would encourage the authors to discuss limitations and impacts of the proposed method, as mentioned in the 'Main Review'.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:  
The authors introduce a self-supervised framework for learning video representations directly from compressed streams. By exploiting both RGB frames and motion vectors without full decompression, the approach aims to cut down on storage requirements and inference time.

Main Review:

Strengths:  
- The method capitalizes on native RGB data and motion vectors in compressed videos, avoiding the overhead of full decoding and thereby enabling efficient representation learning.  
- Empirical results demonstrate that the proposed scheme lowers overall inference latency and storage footprint, while delivering a slight performance edge over existing state-of-the-art techniques.

Weaknesses:  
- The current design omits any explicit temporal learning objectives—such as shuffling the order of RGB frames or motion vectors within a clip (as done in IMRNet)—which could further enrich the learned video features.  
- Architecturally, the model relies primarily on the InfoNCE loss; aside from varying the selection of positive and negative pairs for RGB and motion vectors, there is little diversification in the loss functions applied.  
- According to Table 1, a significant portion of the computational burden stems from preprocessing. Although not a direct apples-to-apples comparison, employing parallel re-encoding as in IMRNet might substantially reduce this overhead.

Limitations and Societal Impact:  
I recommend that the authors include a dedicated discussion on the method’s limitations and potential societal repercussions, as noted under the “Main Review” section.

reviews.summary: The authors propose a self-supervised learning (SSL) method for learning strong visual representations based on compressed videos. Compared with prior work, which sees videos in RGB only, the proposed method is more efficient and leverages the motion information that's already accessible in compressed videos. The authors propose to use a contrastive learning method to supervise on kind of modality with correspondence of another modality. On multiple datasets (mainly UCF and HMDB), good performance is achieved, with both linear probing and transfer learning settings.
reviews.main_review:
[originality: medium-high]
 - The idea of using compressed videos for self-supervised learning is novel/original. Most prior work that operates on compressed videos focues on supervised learning setting only. The implementation of this idea is standard/expected (this is not a drawback).

[quality: medium-high]
 - The paper covers useful ablation study that I'd expect. In particular, the authors demonstrate the usefulness of the compressed-video modality (MV) compared to RGB only (Table 3). Compared to prior work, the performance is also strong while being significantly more efficient. The ablation study that compares with optical flow and low-res optical flow is also interesting (Table 2).

[clarity: high]
 - I think this paper is overall clearly writen. One question about implementation details: How is the similarity calculated in the hard-positive mining procedure?

[significance: medium-high]
 - The area of SSL draws a lot of attention in our community and is of great potential. I think this work extends our knowledge from a new dimension with the use of compressed videos. I think this work can lead to promising new directions in SSL research.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
1. In the paper, the authors perform experiments/evaluation solely on videos. I was wondering if the method learns good representations for "image" tasks as well. Namely, leveraging MV can potentially give us better RGB representations for images, too. I wonder what the authors think about this.
2. If the hard-positive mining is based on the learned features, would there be any "chichen-and-egg" problem introduced by the proposed procedure?
3. I like the comparison to MV_{CoViAR}, but I wonder how each specific compression setting affects the results. For example, how does GOP size, the use of B-frame vs P-frame, etc. affect results.

Regarding societal impact, I don't see anything particularly concerning (or more concerning) than other SSL work.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Summary:  
This paper introduces a self-supervised learning framework that taps directly into compressed video streams—specifically motion vectors (MV) and residuals—instead of relying solely on decoded RGB frames. By building a contrastive learning objective that treats one compressed modality as a “positive” target for another, the authors exploit motion information already present in compressed formats, yielding both stronger representations and greater computational efficiency. Evaluations on UCF101 and HMDB51 under linear-probing and transfer-learning protocols show that the proposed approach matches or exceeds prior RGB-only methods while running significantly faster.

Originality (medium–high):  
– Leveraging compressed-video modalities for SSL is a fresh direction; prior work using compressed data has focused on fully supervised scenarios.  
– The overall design—applying contrastive learning across modalities—is straightforward but appropriately chosen for this setting.

Quality (medium–high):  
– A solid ablation in Table 3 confirms that adding MV to RGB improves performance over RGB-only SSL.  
– Efficiency gains are clear: the method runs much faster than baselines, with no accuracy drop.  
– The comparison to optical flow—and even to low-resolution flow—in Table 2 is informative and shows that compressed motion vectors serve as a competitive, cheaper alternative.

Clarity (high):  
– The manuscript is generally well written and easy to follow.  
– One detail I’d appreciate more on: in the hard‐positive mining step, how exactly is the similarity between features computed?

Significance (medium–high):  
– Self-supervised video representation learning is a hot topic, and demonstrating strong SSL from compressed streams opens up a promising new research avenue.  
– This work could inspire further investigations into other modalities embedded in compressed data or extend to hybrid SSL frameworks.

Limitations and Questions:  
1. All experiments focus on video classification. Could the representations learned from compressed streams also benefit still‐image tasks? Might MV-enhanced pretraining improve pure image benchmarks?  
2. The hard-positive mining relies on features that are themselves evolving—does this introduce any circularity or “chicken‐and‐egg” issue in training?  
3. You compare against MVCoViAR, which is helpful, but I’d like to know how different compression parameters (GOP size, ratio of B-frames to P-frames, etc.) influence the results.

Societal Impact:  
I do not see any unusual ethical or societal risks beyond those typical for general SSL research.

Rating: 7 (Good paper; accept)

reviews.summary: The paper tackles the problem of self-supervised video representation learning. The authors propose a compressed video based self-supervised learning approach, where the model is forced to learn matching representations between raw RGB clip and its corresponding motion vector clip. This is achieved using a relatively standard contrastive learning formulation. The authors then demonstrate that their model achieves state-of-the-art performance on a few downstream tasks (i.e., action recognition, and video retrieval) while also being significantly more efficient (particularly in terms of the video processing time).
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:
- The paper is written clearly, and it addresses a well motivated and an important research problem.
- A nice idea of using compressed video for self-supervised feature learning.
- The proposed approach delivers performance that is comparable/better to previous methods (e.g. CoCLR [Han et al., 2020b]) while being much more efficient.
- Thorough ablation studies.

Weaknesses:
- The major weakness of this paper is its limited technical novelty. While the idea of using compressed videos in the context of self-supervised learning is new and interesting, overall the proposed approach is largely built on the ideas from CoCLR [Han et al., 2020b]. Having said this, the proposed approach seems to be both more effective and more efficient than CoCLR. Therefore, I think this would be a good "poster" paper.
- The proposed approach performs quite a bit worse than the recent CVPR 2021 methods such as CVRL, and especially the method of Feichtenhofer et al. "A Large-Scale Study on Unsupervised Spatiotemporal Representation Learning" (CVPR 2021). Having said this, given a very large computational cost of both of these prior methods (i.e. 800 epoch training), I think that it would be unfair to compare the proposed MVCGC approach directly with these methods. The authors should still add the second reference to their related work section, and also to their quantitative comparisons. A discussion on why the direct comparison between these methods is unfair would also be useful.

Questions / Minor Details:
- In L85-88, the authors mention that unlike CoCLR, they construct sample pairs between different views of different clip (i.e., RGB frames of one clip and motion vectors of another clip from the same video). However, the caption of Fig.3 says that the motion vector clip corresponds to the raw RGB clip. Therefore, I was confused, which one is it? Also, if it's the former, could the authors also ablate the latter?
- Typo in Table 3 (it should be Retrieval instead of Retrival). 
- In the third part of Table 4, the authors highlight/bold the numbers corresponding to their approach even though CVRL achieves higher accuracy.
- If the authors could include the linear evaluation on Kinetics-400 (similar to "A Large-Scale Study on Unsupervised Spatiotemporal Representation Learning") that would be great. I think the evaluation on UCF and HMDB is a bit saturated and outdated. Therefore, it would be useful to have additional numbers on Kinetics, which is a bit more challenging dataset.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitations were not discussed.

6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This manuscript addresses self-supervised video representation learning by leveraging compressed video streams. The authors enforce alignment between representations of raw RGB clips and their corresponding motion vector clips using a contrastive learning framework. Experiments demonstrate that this method matches or surpasses previous approaches on downstream tasks (action recognition and video retrieval) while offering substantial gains in processing efficiency.

Strengths:
- The paper is clearly written and tackles a well-motivated, important problem.
- Introducing compressed video (motion vectors) as a self-supervision signal is an appealing and novel idea.
- The proposed model achieves performance on par with or better than earlier work—e.g., CoCLR [Han et al., 2020b]—while requiring much less computation.
- Extensive ablation studies are provided to validate design choices.

Weaknesses:
- The technical innovation beyond CoCLR is modest. Although applying compressed-video inputs to self-supervised learning is interesting, the core contrastive framework closely follows CoCLR’s methodology. That said, the authors’ version appears both more accurate and more efficient, which makes it suited for a poster contribution.
- The performance lags behind recent CVPR 2021 efforts such as CVRL and particularly Feichtenhofer et al.’s “A Large-Scale Study on Unsupervised Spatiotemporal Representation Learning.” However, given that those methods rely on very heavy training regimes (e.g., 800 epochs), a direct apples-to-apples comparison may be misleading. I recommend citing Feichtenhofer et al. in the related work, including their numbers in tables, and adding a discussion explaining why a head-to-head comparison is not entirely fair.

Questions and Minor Points:
- In lines 85–88, the text states that, unlike CoCLR, sample pairs come from different clips (RGB frames of one clip matched with motion vectors from another clip in the same video). Yet Fig. 3’s caption implies the motion vector clip corresponds directly to the raw RGB clip. Could the authors clarify which setup is used? If both variants exist, an ablation comparing them would be insightful.
- Typographical error in Table 3: “Retrival” should read “Retrieval.”
- In the third block of Table 4, results for the proposed method are bolded despite CVRL achieving higher accuracy; this highlighting is misleading.
- It would strengthen the evaluation to include a linear probe on Kinetics-400 (following Feichtenhofer et al.), since UCF101 and HMDB51 are becoming saturated benchmarks. Kinetics results would offer a more challenging, up-to-date comparison.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
- The manuscript does not discuss any limitations or broader social impacts of the proposed method.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper presents a self-supervised algorithm for representation learning of videos. The authors propose using RGB clips and Motion Vectors (MV) available in compressed videos to reduce the computational burden of processing. The two streams are seen as views in the contrastive setting: positive pairs are composed of RGB and MV clips of the same video, negative ones have clips belonging to different samples. Moreover, hard positive mining extends the set of positive pairs by seeking other videos that are similar in both views. The approach compares favorably on action datasets UC101 and HMDB51 for both action recognition and video retrieval tasks.
reviews.main_review:
## Strenghts
* The contribution of this paper is timely: the computational requirements of contrastive learning hinders its applicability on video representation learning. Compressed videos enables the shrinkage of required time and storage.
* The work proves how effective use of the less informative MV is on par with the computationally demanding optical flow
* Extensive experiments on action recognition and retrieval prove that the proposed gets competitive results with state of the art methods: linear evaluation and full fine-tuning accuracy for action recognition and R@k metrics for retrieval.

## Weaknesses
* The method builds on [1] by extending the single-view contrastive approach to learn the correspondence between two views and changing the hard-positive condition. However, it is well known that cross-view contrastive is beneficial [2, 3, 4]. Yet, the showed technique for harvesting hard positives is an incremental contribution to the literature.
* How cross guidance and hard positive mining contribute to the final performance is not well disentangled and it's not easy to understand what their impact is. What's the performance loss without hard-positives? What is the effect of using hard positives in the initialization phase? What is the performance of the ensemble of RGB and MV model right after initialization?

### Minor
* Table 2: I find somewhat surprising that the same technique applied to OF gets lower performances, could the authors provide some explanations/insights?
* Line 258: concerning the claim "MVCGC is robust to motion vectors under different encoding configurations": is not really clear how this is proved. In table 2 MV_CoViAr is used for initialization only.
* Line 303: I think that it is "by retrieval with only" RGB "are even higher"
* Appendix, fig 1: the underlying assumptions is that semantically similar videos are close in both RGB and MV space. How do the authors think the model would handle failures of such assumption, e.g., mopping a gymnastic floor, biking in a volleyball arena, etc.?
--------------------
* **Correctness:** The proposed method and the experimental evaluation are sound.
* **Clarity:** The paper is well written and easy to follow. The content is well organized: contribution is expressed concisely and the experimental part is well explained.
* **Relation to prior work:** Authors relates their work with previous literature while concisely highlighting their contribution.
* **Reproducibility:** Architecture, method and optimization hyperparameters are available for implementation, missing augmentation parameters (random cropping, blurring, jittering) and missing code limits the reproducibility of the work though.
* **Originality**: The contribution of this paper is incremental with respect to cited papers, the real advantages of the technique are not thoroughly highlighted in the ablation part.
------------------------
[1] Han, Tengda, Weidi Xie, and Andrew Zisserman. "Self-supervised Co-Training for Video Representation Learning." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 33 (2020): 5679-5690.

[2] Tian, Yonglong, Dilip Krishnan, and Phillip Isola. "Contrastive multiview coding." Computer Vision–ECCV 2020: 16th European Conference, Glasgow, UK, August 23–28, 2020, Proceedings, Part XI 16. Springer International Publishing, 2020.

[3] Bachman, Philip, R. Devon Hjelm, and William Buchwalter. "Learning Representations by Maximizing Mutual Information Across Views." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 32 (2019): 15535-15545.

[4] Chen, Ting, et al. "A simple framework for contrastive learning of visual representations." International conference on machine learning. PMLR, 2020.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: N/A. However, I would argue that no specific discussion is needed on potential negative societal impact for a contrastive learning method.
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

[1] Han, Tengda, Weidi Xie, and Andrew Zisserman. "Self-supervised Co-Training for Video Representation Learning." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 33 (2020): 5679-5690.

[2] Tian, Yonglong, Dilip Krishnan, and Phillip Isola. "Contrastive multiview coding." Computer Vision–ECCV 2020: 16th European Conference, Glasgow, UK, August 23–28, 2020, Proceedings, Part XI 16. Springer International Publishing, 2020.

[3] Bachman, Philip, R. Devon Hjelm, and William Buchwalter. "Learning Representations by Maximizing Mutual Information Across Views." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 32 (2019): 15535-15545.

[4] Chen, Ting, et al. "A simple framework for contrastive learning of visual representations." International conference on machine learning. PMLR, 2020.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
N/A. However, I would argue that no specific discussion is needed on potential negative societal impact for a contrastive learning method.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
Summary:
This manuscript introduces a self-supervised video representation learning method that leverages both RGB frames and Motion Vectors (MV) extracted from compressed video streams to lower computational and storage overheads. The two modalities are treated as distinct “views” in a contrastive framework: positive pairs consist of an RGB clip and its corresponding MV clip, while negatives are drawn from different video samples. To enrich positives, the authors propose a hard‐positive mining strategy that finds other videos exhibiting similarity in both RGB and MV spaces. Experiments on UCF101 and HMDB51 demonstrate competitive results on action recognition (linear evaluation and full fine-tuning) and video retrieval (R@k metrics).

Strengths:
• Timeliness: Tackles the high computational demands of contrastive learning for video by exploiting readily available compressed video data.  
• Efficiency: Shows that MVs—although less informative than optical flow—can match its performance while being far cheaper to compute.  
• Thorough evaluation: Provides extensive action recognition and retrieval experiments, comparing favorably with state-of-the-art methods.

Weaknesses:
• Incremental novelty: The work extends the single-view contrastive approach of [1] into a cross-view setting and adjusts the hard-positive definition. However, cross-view contrastive learning is already well established [2,3,4], and the hard-positive mining strategy represents only a modest advancement.  
• Ablation clarity: It remains unclear exactly how much cross-stream guidance and hard-positive mining each contribute. For instance:  
  – What is the performance penalty if hard positives are omitted altogether?  
  – How much benefit arises from using hard positives only during initialization?  
  – What is the accuracy of the combined RGB+MV model immediately after the initialization phase, before full training?

Minor Comments:
1. Table 2 reports that applying the same hard-positive technique to optical flow underperforms relative to MVs—could the authors offer insight into this discrepancy?  
2. On line 258, the claim “MVCGC is robust to motion vectors under different encoding configurations” is not clearly supported, since in Table 2 MV_CoViAr appears only in the initialization stage.  
3. On line 303, I suggest revising to “by retrieval with only RGB are even higher.”  
4. In Appendix Figure 1, the method assumes semantically similar videos are close in both RGB and MV feature spaces. How would the model handle cases where this assumption fails (e.g., mopping a gymnastics mat vs. cleaning a volleyball court)?

Evaluation:
• Correctness: The method and experiments are sound.  
• Clarity: The paper is well written and logically organized; ideas and results are clearly explained.  
• Relation to prior work: The authors situate their contribution within existing literature and articulate their additions.  
• Reproducibility: Architectural and optimization details are provided, but missing augmentation parameters (random cropping, blurring, jittering) and unavailable code hinder full reproducibility.  
• Originality: The contributions are incremental over prior cross-view contrastive methods, and the benefits are not fully dissected in the ablation studies.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
No specific societal risks are identified; contrastive learning for video representation carries no evident negative impact in this context.

Decision:
4: Ok but not good enough – rejection
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
This paper presents a self-supervised algorithm for representation learning of videos. The authors propose using RGB clips and Motion Vectors (MV) available in compressed videos to reduce the computational burden of processing. The two streams are seen as views in the contrastive setting: positive pairs are composed of RGB and MV clips of the same video, negative ones have clips belonging to different samples. Moreover, hard positive mining extends the set of positive pairs by seeking other videos that are similar in both views. The approach compares favorably on action datasets UC101 and HMDB51 for both action recognition and video retrieval tasks.

Main Review:
## Strenghts
* The contribution of this paper is timely: the computational requirements of contrastive learning hinders its applicability on video representation learning. Compressed videos enables the shrinkage of required time and storage.
* The work proves how effective use of the less informative MV is on par with the computationally demanding optical flow
* Extensive experiments on action recognition and retrieval prove that the proposed gets competitive results with state of the art methods: linear evaluation and full fine-tuning accuracy for action recognition and R@k metrics for retrieval.

## Weaknesses
* The method builds on [1] by extending the single-view contrastive approach to learn the correspondence between two views and changing the hard-positive condition. However, it is well known that cross-view contrastive is beneficial [2, 3, 4]. Yet, the showed technique for harvesting hard positives is an incremental contribution to the literature.
* How cross guidance and hard positive mining contribute to the final performance is not well disentangled and it's not easy to understand what their impact is. What's the performance loss without hard-positives? What is the effect of using hard positives in the initialization phase? What is the performance of the ensemble of RGB and MV model right after initialization?

### Minor
* Table 2: I find somewhat surprising that the same technique applied to OF gets lower performances, could the authors provide some explanations/insights?
* Line 258: concerning the claim "MVCGC is robust to motion vectors under different encoding configurations": is not really clear how this is proved. In table 2 MV_CoViAr is used for initialization only.
* Line 303: I think that it is "by retrieval with only" RGB "are even higher"
* Appendix, fig 1: the underlying assumptions is that semantically similar videos are close in both RGB and MV space. How do the authors think the model would handle failures of such assumption, e.g., mopping a gymnastic floor, biking in a volleyball arena, etc.?
--------------------
* **Correctness:** The proposed method and the experimental evaluation are sound.
* **Clarity:** The paper is well written and easy to follow. The content is well organized: contribution is expressed concisely and the experimental part is well explained.
* **Relation to prior work:** Authors relates their work with previous literature while concisely highlighting their contribution.
* **Reproducibility:** Architecture, method and optimization hyperparameters are available for implementation, missing augmentation parameters (random cropping, blurring, jittering) and missing code limits the reproducibility of the work though.
* **Originality**: The contribution of this paper is incremental with respect to cited papers, the real advantages of the technique are not thoroughly highlighted in the ablation part.
------------------------
[1] Han, Tengda, Weidi Xie, and Andrew Zisserman. "Self-supervised Co-Training for Video Representation Learning." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 33 (2020): 5679-5690.

[2] Tian, Yonglong, Dilip Krishnan, and Phillip Isola. "Contrastive multiview coding." Computer Vision–ECCV 2020: 16th European Conference, Glasgow, UK, August 23–28, 2020, Proceedings, Part XI 16. Springer International Publishing, 2020.

[3] Bachman, Philip, R. Devon Hjelm, and William Buchwalter. "Learning Representations by Maximizing Mutual Information Across Views." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 32 (2019): 15535-15545.

[4] Chen, Ting, et al. "A simple framework for contrastive learning of visual representations." International conference on machine learning. PMLR, 2020.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
N/A. However, I would argue that no specific discussion is needed on potential negative societal impact for a contrastive learning method.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.original_rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection

title: Unifying Gradient Estimators for Meta-Reinforcement Learning  via Off-Policy Evaluation

reviews.summary:
The paper uses the recently developed framework of Taylor expansions for value functions (ref [29] in the paper), expands the results, and shows how they could be applied to Off-Policy Evaluation, and by a non-trivial extension to meta-learning. The main algorthmic contribution of the paper is the application of the ideas to meta-learning, but I feel the main contribution is a conceptual one, further developing the ideas from [29] and drawing theoretical connections between OPE and meta-learning.

The paper includes an experimental section demonstrating marginal improvement over baselines in several domains.
reviews.main_review:
Overview: Despite some flaws, I think this is a good paper and leaning towards acceptance. I think the two main limitations of the paper are:
(1) It draws very heavily on the theory and novelty of [29] and therefore its own novelty is a bit limited.
(2) The empirical improvements obtained for meta-learning (the problem the paper sets out to advance) are limited.
Additionally, there are some changes that should be made to make the paper clearer, but I think those can be easily fixed for the camera ready if the paper is accepted. Despite the drawbacks, the reason I support acceptance is that I feel the additional contributions on top of the already interesting results of [29] are non-trivial, and this is a potentially interesting research direction which could provide insight into many aspects of RL. For example, viewing meta-learning through the lens of Taylor expansions for value functions allows for a very clear connection and unification of derivatives estimations of value functions and OPE.

A general suggestion for the authors which I feel might improve the overall story of the paper, is to lean more heavily on the theoretical results and their ability to shed light on interesting insights such as the connection between OPE and meta-learning, and OPE to high order derivatives. At least my personal experience reading the paper was expecting significant novelty and improvement of results on meta-learning, which set me up for a disappointment. It was only on a second read that I appreciated the theoretical insights in the first half of the paper more. Additionally, I would set up the connection with [29] more - the authors do a good enough job of properly citing [29], but not enough to make it clear that the reader should be familiar with [29] to fully appreciate this paper. After emphasizing the theory and insights, I think the meta-learning algorithm and result can be presented as a cool implication (this is obviously a very subjective suggestion).

Some more specific comments:
- In lines 120-122 regarding how the approximation maintains the higher-order dependencies on \theta. I feel like I'm missing something and the sentence is failing to convey an idea it is trying to. Obviously when you make an approximation in some parameter you maintain higher order terms in the small parameter, and choose how many terms to keep. So this statement seems trivial and I'd like to know if there's something deeper which the authors are trying to convey and I'm missing.
- In section 3.2 and Proposition 3.1 - Is K a typo and should be m? (if yes, it is a consistent typo, which is why I'm confused). If it isn't a typo, I couldn't see where K is defined, and also can't follow the proof of Proportion 3.1, so this is a point I would really like clarification on.
- In section 5.1, the authors claim to investigate the bias/variance tradeoff of their method, but only show results for the overall MSE of estimating the derivative. I think the authors should show explicit plots of the decomposed variance and bias of each estimator shown.
- very minor - the y-labels in Figure 2 a-b are cut.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
The authors do a good job of discussing the limitations of their method compared to alternative methods.

The paper is mostly theoretical and I don't see a potential negative soceital impact.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Summary:  
This manuscript builds on the Taylor expansion approach for value functions introduced in [29], extends those theoretical results, and illustrates how they can be used for off-policy evaluation (OPE) as well as, via a non-trivial extension, for meta-learning. Although the chief algorithmic novelty is the meta-learning application, the paper’s real advance is conceptual—deepening the framework of [29] and forging theoretical ties between OPE and meta-learning. An experimental section reports modest improvements over standard baselines across multiple domains.

Main Review:  
Overall, I view this as a solid paper and am inclined to accept it. That said, it has two principal weaknesses:  
1. It leans heavily on the theoretical machinery and originality of [29], limiting its own novelty.  
2. The empirical gains on the meta-learning task—which is presented as the main algorithmic contribution—are quite small.

There are also several clarity issues that, in my view, could be addressed easily in a camera-ready version if accepted. In spite of these drawbacks, I recommend acceptance because the additional theoretical developments beyond [29] are non-trivial, and this direction of research could shed new light on various aspects of reinforcement learning. For instance, interpreting meta-learning through the lens of Taylor expansions for value functions unifies derivative estimators of value functions with OPE in a compelling way.

General Suggestion:  
I encourage the authors to foreground the theoretical contributions and the novel insights they offer—such as the linkage between OPE and meta-learning, and the extension to higher-order derivatives. My initial reading set my expectations toward major empirical advances in meta-learning, which left me underwhelmed until a second pass revealed the depth of the theory in the first half. Moreover, I suggest making the relationship with [29] more explicit: the current citations are adequate, but it isn’t clear that readers should be well versed in [29] to fully appreciate this work. Once the theory and its implications are clearly established, the meta-learning algorithm and results can be presented as an appealing by-product of those insights.

Specific Comments:  
- Lines 120–122: The claim that the approximation “maintains the higher-order dependencies on θ” reads as trivial. Every approximation in a small parameter retains higher-order terms up to the chosen order, so I suspect the authors mean something subtler here—please clarify.  
- Section 3.2 and Proposition 3.1: Is “K” a typo for “m”? If not, I cannot locate where K is defined, and the proof of Proposition 3.1 is hard to follow without that definition.  
- Section 5.1: The discussion promises an analysis of bias–variance trade-offs, yet only overall MSE curves are shown. It would strengthen the paper to include separate plots for the bias and variance of each estimator.  
- Minor: In Figure 2(a) and (b), the y-axis labels are cut off.

Limitations and Societal Impact:  
The authors adequately acknowledge the limitations of their approach relative to alternative methods. As the work is largely theoretical, I do not foresee any adverse societal impact.

Rating:  
7: Good paper, accept
