reviews.summary: This paper considers meta-RL with gradient-based adaptations, which relies heavily on the estimation of the Hessians of value functions. Though there are previous approaches that focus on unbiased/biased estimations of Hessian. This paper gives a unified view for estimating higher-order derivatives of value functions, through the lens of off-policy evaluation. This subsumes several previous works, also sheds some light on the bias-variance tradeoff of the estimate. Empirically, the new proposed second-order estimate is also incorporated in meta-RL algorithms to showcase the empirical efficiency.
reviews.main_review:
(Originality): The main contributions I see from this paper are: (1). a unified framework for estimating higher-order derivatives of value functions, through OPE methods. (2). the use of Taylor expansion in the value function, which controls the number of IS ratios in the value estimate, hence sheds some light on the bias-variance trade-off. (3). also a new method arising from this framework.

(Quality): The motivation is clear, and the method is clearly stated. Some comments here include:
[1]. For the bias-variance trade-off with the Taylor expansion, how to select the optimal $K$? Also, how does this bias-variance trade-off translate into the downstream meta-gradient estimations? Any comments on this?
[2]. In experiment section 5.1, the bias-variance trade-off examination seems a little bit confusing to me. It seems the argument here is the second-order method achieves a better trade-off through high correlation with the ground truth. Is there any way to empirically examine the bias and variance in this tabular setting?
[3]. For the continuous control problem, it seems the improvement from the new proposed approach is pretty marginal since the bias might be a dominating issue here. Are there any environments/MDPs/settings that we can expect a larger improvement of it? It would be great to include some discussions/new experiments on this.

(Clarity): The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Limitations are discussed in Section 3, while it seems it does not have any societal impact.

Main Review:

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This manuscript tackles meta-reinforcement learning methods that rely on gradient-based adaptation, which in turn depend critically on estimating the Hessian of the value function. While prior work has proposed both unbiased and biased Hessian estimators, this paper offers a unifying perspective on computing higher-order derivatives of value functions using tools from off-policy evaluation. In doing so, it subsumes several existing techniques and provides insights into the bias–variance balance of these estimators. Finally, the authors integrate their novel second-order estimator into meta-RL algorithms and present empirical results demonstrating its practical benefits.

Originality:
The paper’s key innovations include:
1. A cohesive off-policy evaluation framework for deriving higher-order value-function derivatives.
2. The application of a Taylor-series expansion in the value estimate to limit the count of importance-sampling ratios, thereby clarifying the associated bias–variance trade-off.
3. A concrete new second-order estimation method that emerges naturally from this general framework.

Quality:
The motivation behind the work is well articulated, and the methodology is explained in a clear and logical manner. I have a few suggestions and questions:
[1] Regarding the bias–variance trade-off introduced via the Taylor expansion: how should one choose the optimal order K in practice? Additionally, could the authors comment on how this trade-off impacts the final meta-gradient estimates?
[2] In Section 5.1’s experiments, the evaluation of bias versus variance is somewhat hard to interpret. The narrative appears to hinge on the second-order approach showing higher correlation with ground truth. Could the authors propose or include direct empirical measurements of bias and variance within this tabular domain?
[3] In the continuous-control experiments, the gains from the proposed estimator seem rather modest—potentially because bias continues to dominate. Are there other environments or MDP configurations where you would expect the method’s advantages to be more pronounced? It would strengthen the paper to discuss such settings or to add experiments illustrating a larger performance gap.

Clarity:
Overall, the exposition is crisp and the paper is easy to follow.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
The authors address the method’s limitations in Section 3, and there do not appear to be any direct societal implications.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: The paper is concerned with meta reinforcement learning. The author(s) introduced a framework for learning high-order derivatives of value functions using off-policy evaluation. Specifically, the author(s) proposed to adopt existing off-policy evaluation methods to derive the value estimator and then take the derivative with respect to the target parameters to construct the estimate. This technique is further adopted to implement meta reinforcement learning.
reviews.main_review:
I have a few major comments regarding the proposed procedure.

1. The author(s) proposed to adopt importance sampling and doubly-robust estimators to evaluate the value function. These estimators suffer from the curse of horizon, leading to the sub-optimality of the learned optimal policy. Given that the author(s) considered an MDP setting, there are more efficient estimators for value evaluation (see, e.g., https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.12429; https://arxiv.org/abs/1909.05850).

2. Instead of constructing estimators for the value, it might be more efficient to directly evaluate the gradient of the value and its Hessian matrix. In the single agent setting, there are efficient off-policy policy gradient estimators developed in the literature (see, e.g., https://arxiv.org/abs/2002.04014).

3. I think the author(s) focused on offline meta reinforcement learning problems. It remains unclear to me how the proposed method would work when the positivity assumption (e.g., the policy ratio shall be bounded away from infinity) in the offline dataset is violated. In the single agent setting, the pessimism principle is commonly adopted to mitigate this issue. Would it be useful to combine the pessimism principle with the proposed method?

reviews.confidence: 1: Your assessment is an educated guess. The submission is not in your area or the submission was difficult to understand. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper is concerned with meta reinforcement learning. The author(s) introduced a framework for learning high-order derivatives of value functions using off-policy evaluation. Specifically, the author(s) proposed to adopt existing off-policy evaluation methods to derive the value estimator and then take the derivative with respect to the target parameters to construct the estimate. This technique is further adopted to implement meta reinforcement learning.

Main Review:
I have a few major comments regarding the proposed procedure.

1. The author(s) proposed to adopt importance sampling and doubly-robust estimators to evaluate the value function. These estimators suffer from the curse of horizon, leading to the sub-optimality of the learned optimal policy. Given that the author(s) considered an MDP setting, there are more efficient estimators for value evaluation (see, e.g., https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.12429; https://arxiv.org/abs/1909.05850).

2. Instead of constructing estimators for the value, it might be more efficient to directly evaluate the gradient of the value and its Hessian matrix. In the single agent setting, there are efficient off-policy policy gradient estimators developed in the literature (see, e.g., https://arxiv.org/abs/2002.04014).

3. I think the author(s) focused on offline meta reinforcement learning problems. It remains unclear to me how the proposed method would work when the positivity assumption (e.g., the policy ratio shall be bounded away from infinity) in the offline dataset is violated. In the single agent setting, the pessimism principle is commonly adopted to mitigate this issue. Would it be useful to combine the pessimism principle with the proposed method?

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The authors have discussed the limitations of their work on Page 6.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This manuscript tackles meta-reinforcement learning by developing a strategy to compute higher-order derivatives of value functions via off-policy evaluation. Concretely, the authors employ established off-policy evaluation techniques—specifically importance sampling and doubly robust estimators—to form a value function estimate, then differentiate this estimate with respect to the target parameters. They extend this methodology to the meta-RL setting.

Major Comments:

1. Reliance on importance sampling and doubly robust estimators incurs the well-known “curse of horizon,” which can degrade the quality of the resulting policy. Since the work assumes an MDP framework, one could leverage more sample-efficient value estimators tailored to MDPs (see, for example, https://arxiv.org/abs/1810.12429 and https://arxiv.org/abs/1909.05850).

2. Rather than first estimating the value and then differentiating, it may be more effective to directly estimate the policy gradient and Hessian of the value function. The literature on single-agent off-policy policy gradient methods offers efficient estimators for these derivatives (e.g., https://arxiv.org/abs/2002.04014).

3. The paper appears to address purely offline meta-RL scenarios. It is unclear how the proposed framework behaves if the positivity assumption (i.e., bounded importance ratios) is violated in the offline data. In single-agent offline RL, practitioners often employ pessimism-based approaches to handle such violations. Would integrating a pessimism principle into your method improve robustness?

Limitations and Societal Impact:
The authors acknowledge the limitations of their approach in Section 6 (page 6).

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

Original Decision:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper uses Huang and Jiang (2020) to talk about gradient-based meta learning in reinforcement learning using the language of off-policy evaluation. The logical connection behind those concepts stands from the fact that policy gradient methods in reinforcement learning are in fact instances of the likelihood ratio derivative estimator, which itself stems from important sampling. Proposition 3.2 and 3.3 are obtained from Huang and Jiang. Proposition 3.1 tries to extend their results to talk about higher order derivatives. The author's finally propose to use ideas from Taylor policy optimization from Tang et al. (2020) as a variance reduction method within the doubly robust policy gradient framework of Huang and Jiang.
reviews.main_review:
In my opinion the paper doesn't go deep enough into the individual ideas being put forward. The doubly robust estimator of Huang and Jiang is at the core of this paper.  In the specific context of meta learning, what does it allow us to say or do that we couldn't achieve before? Why is doubly robust necessary? The doubly robust being a variance reduction technique why this VRT in particular and not another one, in the meta learning context? Regarding the Taylor approximation, why is it related to the doubly robust estimator? The paper does not do a convincing job at showing that these two components strongly depend on each other: conceptually, or experimentally. Overall, the paper lacks cohesion and depth.

On line 83 the notation suggests that the expectation is not taken over x and only over the task g. While technically correct, you don't really have to write the objective as an expectation over the value function. Your objective should simply be the expected discounted or on discounted return, written plainly as a sum of rewards.

In equation 2, I would suggest using a different notation when you're writing nabla of theta prime. Theta prime should be a function, but the way that it's written suggests that it's a variable instead.

Another suggestion for notation would be to write "v of theta" (instead of a superscript) in equation 2. This way it would be clearer how you have to apply the chain rule later on.

Reading more carefully on line 92 and equation 1 now I realize that you assume a fixed initial distribution starting from x0. This is confusing because on line 83 you simply write v of x and it looks like x should be a random variable: your initial state. Perhaps you should state explicitly that you assume (without loss of generality) a given initial state.

On line 92, the expression the "Hessian of the value function" is misleading because really the Hessian that you're taking is that of the expected return objective, not that of the value function.

I think that the unnumbered equation below line 106 is redundant: it simply says that the two quantities are unbiased estimators. Words suffice here. Instead, I would suggest that you explain the nature of the issue. The correlation problem that you're describing comes from taking the expectation over a product of two random variables. The fact that it's not simply the product of the individual expectations and that here is also a covariance term.

I'm confused by proposition 3.1 especially around line 137 when you introduce a new notation for the estimator nabla hat v. What you have in this proposition should be something about a derivative estimator: score function/REINFORCE, reparametrization. Now you don't seem to be defining how you obtain this nabla theta m  v hat, and in that sense proposition 3.1 does not quite stand on its own.

On line 171 you allude to the fact that the variance of policy learning is essentially due to having a large number of importance sampling terms. While this is a reasonable intuition to have, note that we can show formally that PDIS-type estimators need not have larger variance than let's say stationary or marginal IS.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The limitation of the framework per se are not quite explored. The authors talk about what can be said within that framework (dice) but not quite what it cannot say. Experimentally the paper looks at the performance of the combination of doubly robust plus Taylor approximation as an algorithm of its own. It does not quite try to understand what the doubly robust formulation allows us to address specifically in meta learning. It would be interesting to gain more insights into the value of the proposed method by taking a more ablative approach. Since the authors pitch a framework as the main contribution, performance results matter much less than experimental insights at the conceptual level.  I think that the paper could be restructured around the algorithm and to de-emphasize the framework part. To go all in on the algorithm (which might be how things happened chronologically anyways).
reviews.rating: 4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.confidence: 5: You are absolutely certain about your assessment. You are very familiar with the related work and checked the math/other details carefully.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper uses Huang and Jiang (2020) to talk about gradient-based meta learning in reinforcement learning using the language of off-policy evaluation. The logical connection behind those concepts stands from the fact that policy gradient methods in reinforcement learning are in fact instances of the likelihood ratio derivative estimator, which itself stems from important sampling. Proposition 3.2 and 3.3 are obtained from Huang and Jiang. Proposition 3.1 tries to extend their results to talk about higher order derivatives. The author's finally propose to use ideas from Taylor policy optimization from Tang et al. (2020) as a variance reduction method within the doubly robust policy gradient framework of Huang and Jiang.

Main Review:
In my opinion the paper doesn't go deep enough into the individual ideas being put forward. The doubly robust estimator of Huang and Jiang is at the core of this paper.  In the specific context of meta learning, what does it allow us to say or do that we couldn't achieve before? Why is doubly robust necessary? The doubly robust being a variance reduction technique why this VRT in particular and not another one, in the meta learning context? Regarding the Taylor approximation, why is it related to the doubly robust estimator? The paper does not do a convincing job at showing that these two components strongly depend on each other: conceptually, or experimentally. Overall, the paper lacks cohesion and depth.

On line 83 the notation suggests that the expectation is not taken over x and only over the task g. While technically correct, you don't really have to write the objective as an expectation over the value function. Your objective should simply be the expected discounted or on discounted return, written plainly as a sum of rewards.

In equation 2, I would suggest using a different notation when you're writing nabla of theta prime. Theta prime should be a function, but the way that it's written suggests that it's a variable instead.

Another suggestion for notation would be to write "v of theta" (instead of a superscript) in equation 2. This way it would be clearer how you have to apply the chain rule later on.

Reading more carefully on line 92 and equation 1 now I realize that you assume a fixed initial distribution starting from x0. This is confusing because on line 83 you simply write v of x and it looks like x should be a random variable: your initial state. Perhaps you should state explicitly that you assume (without loss of generality) a given initial state.

On line 92, the expression the "Hessian of the value function" is misleading because really the Hessian that you're taking is that of the expected return objective, not that of the value function.

I think that the unnumbered equation below line 106 is redundant: it simply says that the two quantities are unbiased estimators. Words suffice here. Instead, I would suggest that you explain the nature of the issue. The correlation problem that you're describing comes from taking the expectation over a product of two random variables. The fact that it's not simply the product of the individual expectations and that here is also a covariance term.

I'm confused by proposition 3.1 especially around line 137 when you introduce a new notation for the estimator nabla hat v. What you have in this proposition should be something about a derivative estimator: score function/REINFORCE, reparametrization. Now you don't seem to be defining how you obtain this nabla theta m  v hat, and in that sense proposition 3.1 does not quite stand on its own.

On line 171 you allude to the fact that the variance of policy learning is essentially due to having a large number of importance sampling terms. While this is a reasonable intuition to have, note that we can show formally that PDIS-type estimators need not have larger variance than let's say stationary or marginal IS.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The limitation of the framework per se are not quite explored. The authors talk about what can be said within that framework (dice) but not quite what it cannot say. Experimentally the paper looks at the performance of the combination of doubly robust plus Taylor approximation as an algorithm of its own. It does not quite try to understand what the doubly robust formulation allows us to address specifically in meta learning. It would be interesting to gain more insights into the value of the proposed method by taking a more ablative approach. Since the authors pitch a framework as the main contribution, performance results matter much less than experimental insights at the conceptual level.  I think that the paper could be restructured around the algorithm and to de-emphasize the framework part. To go all in on the algorithm (which might be how things happened chronologically anyways).

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough - rejection
reviews.text:
Summary:
This work builds on Huang and Jiang (2020) to frame gradient-based meta‐learning in reinforcement learning through the lens of off‐policy evaluation. The authors observe that policy gradient methods can be seen as special cases of the likelihood‐ratio derivative estimator, itself derived from importance sampling. They restate Propositions 3.2 and 3.3 from Huang and Jiang, introduce their own Proposition 3.1 extending those results to higher‐order derivatives, and finally advocate incorporating the Taylor policy optimization approach of Tang et al. (2020) as a variance‐reduction strategy within the doubly robust policy gradient framework.

Main Review:
1. Depth and Cohesion  
   While the doubly robust estimator from Huang and Jiang forms the backbone of this paper, it remains unclear what unique benefits it brings to meta‐learning. Specifically, why is a doubly robust method necessary in this setting, and what new capabilities does it unlock? If variance reduction is the goal, what justifies choosing the Taylor‐based technique over alternative VRTs for meta‐learning? The manuscript does not convincingly demonstrate, either conceptually or via experiments, a tight interdependence between the doubly robust component and the Taylor approximation. As a result, the overall presentation feels fragmented and superficial.

2. Notation and Clarity  
   – On line 83, the objective is written as an expectation only over the task distribution g, suggesting x is fixed. Since the true goal is simply the expected (discounted or undiscounted) return, you could present it directly as a sum of rewards rather than framing it as an expectation over V.  
   – In Equation (2), writing ∇θ′ is misleading because θ′ should denote a function, not a variable. Consider revising the notation.  
   – Also in Equation (2), expressing the value function as v(θ) rather than using a superscript will make the subsequent chain rule application clearer.  
   – On line 92 and in Equation (1), you implicitly assume a fixed starting state x₀, yet earlier you treat x as if it were random. Please state explicitly that, without loss of generality, you take the initial state as given.  
   – Referring to the “Hessian of the value function” (line 92) is imprecise: you are actually differentiating the expected return objective, not the value function itself.

3. Redundancies and Explanations  
   The unnumbered equation below line 106 simply asserts that two estimators are unbiased. This can be conveyed in prose. Instead, it would be more valuable to delve into the source of the correlation issue: namely, that taking the expectation of a product introduces a covariance term, not just the product of expectations.

4. Proposition 3.1  
   The statement of Proposition 3.1 (around line 137) is confusing due to the sudden introduction of the notation \(\hat∇θ^m v\). A proper exposition should describe how this estimator is constructed (e.g., score function/REINFORCE or reparameterization) so that the proposition is self‐contained. As it stands, the result does not stand on its own.

5. Variance Discussion  
   At line 171 you suggest that the high variance in policy learning stems from having many importance‐sampling terms. While this intuition is reasonable, it overlooks formal results showing that PDIS‐type estimators need not exhibit higher variance than stationary or marginal IS approaches. A brief discussion or citation would strengthen this point.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
The authors frame a general theoretical framework (via DICE) but do not explore its boundaries—what it fails to address. Empirically, they demonstrate the performance of combining doubly robust estimation with a Taylor approximation, yet they do not isolate what the doubly robust component specifically contributes in the meta‐learning context. An ablation study or deeper conceptual experiments would yield richer insights. Given that the main selling point is the framework, I recommend reorganizing the paper around the algorithm itself, with less emphasis on the abstract framework and more on the algorithm’s inner workings and empirical behavior.

Rating:
4: Ok but not good enough – rejection

title: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning for Active Voltage Control on Power Distribution Networks

reviews.main_review:
This paper is clearly written and well-organized. The main objective is to present/formulate a problem in power networks as an exciting and yet challenging real-world scenario for application of multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), developing an open-source environment of active voltage control for MARL, conducting some initial empirical study, and summarizing the main observations (including some key observed challenges/limitations of existing MARL techniques in such problem and some potential future research directions). It mainly targets for bridging the gap between the power community and MARL community in this research topic.

I think the problem is interesting and the paper contains valuable results. Bridging the gap between MARL community and power community is important, and the research work here might potentially motivate more future follow-up research in this direction and would also be helpful for real world large scale application of MARL in power networks.

However, for publication of this result on a top MARL/ML conference like NIPS, I'm still not fully convinced by the novelty and contribution of this study. The main contribution of this this paper is more about formulating the active voltage control problem in power network as MARL problem, conducted some initial study of existing MARL techniques in such problem, and identified some key limitations/difficulties there. If the authors could have some results where they addressed some of these identified limitations and made some improvements (which can be somewhat customized to such power network domain) to the existing MARL techniques, then I'd be more convinced that its contribution is sufficient for publication as a regular paper on such a top tier MARL/ML conference like NIPS.

On the other hand, to be clear, I do think this paper indeed studies an interesting problem and it contains valuable research results that might be good for publication at some good conference/journals, and it will be beneficial for future research, especially for interaction between power community and MARL community. But I just think the contribution and novelty in current manuscript might not be strong enough for publication as a regular paper on such a top tier MARL/ML conference like NIPS.

Update: I've read all the review discussions here and checked out all the detailed responses and clarifications from the authors. I'm slightly more convinced about the contribution and novelty of this paper, and I decided to upgrade my rating from 5 to 6. Though I do agree with several other reviewers that, even after the responses and clarifications from the authors, this paper still seems to be quite at the border line.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
In this work, the authors studied applying multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) to active voltage control in the power distribution networks, and targeting for bridging the gap between the power community and MARL community. Main contributions include: 
1) develop an open-source environment of active voltage control for MARL and formally define this problem as a
65 Dec-POMDP;
2) conduct large scale experimentation with 8 state-of-the-art MARL algorithms on different scenarios of voltage control problem;
3) found valuable observations, e.g.,  notice the importance of converting voltage constraints to auxiliary reward functions and verify the sensitivity of MARL performances to the auxiliary reward functions, with a new bowl-shape auxiliary reward proposed which stands out in the test results;
4) by analyzing the experimental results, the authors imply the possible difficulties of applying MARL and the further research directions. The authors also conducted the comparison with traditional control methods, and empirically show the rationality and hope to use MARL as a solution for active voltage control.

reviews.text:
Summary:  
The authors investigate the application of multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) for active voltage regulation in distribution grids, aiming to bridge power systems and MARL research. Their principal contributions are:  
1) The release of an open-source simulation environment for active voltage control and formalizing it as a Dec-POMDP.  
2) Extensive empirical evaluation involving eight state-of-the-art MARL algorithms across varied voltage control scenarios.  
3) Key findings on the necessity of translating voltage constraints into auxiliary reward signals, an analysis of algorithm performance sensitivity to these rewards, and the proposal of a novel bowl-shaped auxiliary reward that outperforms baselines.  
4) A thorough examination of experimental outcomes to highlight challenges in this domain, propose future research directions, and a comparison against classical control techniques to demonstrate MARL’s practical promise.

Detailed Review:  
This manuscript is well‐structured and clearly written. Its goal—to recast active voltage control as a challenging, real-world MARL benchmark, provide an open-source platform, run initial algorithmic studies, and distill critical insights—is timely and useful. By targeting both power engineers and the MARL community, it addresses an important interdisciplinary gap.

Strengths include:  
- The problem selection is compelling and of clear industry relevance.  
- The open-source environment will likely stimulate follow-on work.  
- The large-scale experiments yield valuable observations, especially regarding auxiliary reward design and the effectiveness of the proposed bowl-shaped function.  
- The paper provides a lucid discussion of limitations and outlines promising avenues for future investigation.

However, I have reservations about whether the work meets the bar for a premier MARL/ML conference such as NeurIPS. The core novelty lies primarily in problem formulation, environment development, benchmarking existing algorithms, and identifying their shortcomings. What is missing are concrete algorithmic contributions that directly address the revealed limitations—e.g., tailored modifications to MARL methods or new techniques demonstrating clear performance gains beyond the reward shaping insight. Including such domain-customized improvements would strengthen the case for acceptance as a full regular paper at a top-tier venue.

That said, this study represents a valuable interdisciplinary resource and could be a strong fit for other reputable outlets or workshops focused on bridging power systems and MARL. It will certainly benefit researchers interested in practical, large-scale deployments of MARL in critical infrastructure.

After reviewing the author responses and community discussion, I find the contributions somewhat more compelling than initially thought and have adjusted my score from 5 to 6. Nonetheless, I continue to view the manuscript as borderline for a top-tier conference without additional algorithmic innovations.

Limitations and Societal Impact: Yes  

Rating: 6 (Marginally above the acceptance threshold)

If the authors could have some results where they addressed some of these identified limitations and made some improvements (which can be somewhat customized to such power network domain) to the existing MARL techniques, then I'd be more convinced that its contribution is sufficient for publication as a regular paper on such a top tier MARL/ML conference like NIPS.

On the other hand, to be clear, I do think this paper indeed studies an interesting problem and it contains valuable research results that might be good for publication at some good conference/journals, and it will be beneficial for future research, especially for interaction between power community and MARL community. But I just think the contribution and novelty in current manuscript might not be strong enough for publication as a regular paper on such a top tier MARL/ML conference like NIPS.

Update: I've read all the review discussions here and checked out all the detailed responses and clarifications from the authors. I'm slightly more convinced about the contribution and novelty of this paper, and I decided to upgrade my rating from 5 to 6. Though I do agree with several other reviewers that, even after the responses and clarifications from the authors, this paper still seems to be quite at the border line.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
Yes

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a new environment for multi-agent reinforcement learning that addresses the problem of active voltage control on electric power distribution networks. The authors formulate the voltage control problem as a Dec-POMDP, and implement a number of different MARL baselines to assess their performance on the environment. They also assess the effect of different voltage loss functions on this environment.
reviews.main_review:
I am very much on the borderline regarding this paper. On the one hand, I think it addresses a very important problem, and that it would be extremely beneficial to both the ML/MARL and power systems communities to have this kind of distribution system control environment readily available. On the other hand, I think one of the key assumptions underpinning the current framework needs to be better justified, that there should be more details about the actual usability of the environment, and that some of the technical notations and explanations need to be cleaned up.

Key assumption: One of my main concerns is regarding the assumption made in the paper that multiple inverters are controlled by one company that has full information within its operational region. I do not believe this model is common in present distribution networks -- that is, where smart inverter control exists, it is generally the case that each inverter operates separately based on local information. The authors should provide much more context around this assumption (e.g., how does this grid operation mode relate to existing grid operation modes?), both to avoid inadvertently misleading the ML community about the state of current grid operations, and to better justify the technical assumptions on which the rest of the work is based.

Usage instructions: Since the proposed contribution is an environment with baselines, I was surprised to see no details relating to the practical usability of the environment. For instance, would this environment be integrated within existing MARL frameworks, and/or does it have a user interface that is similar? Is the plan to provide a quickstart guide to help users easily get started? These kinds of details are important to understanding whether this kind of environment would actually be able to be widely adopted, or whether ML researchers/practitioners would find it difficult to use in practice.

Terminology and explanations: As one of the goals of the paper is to make the voltage control setting accessible to ML practitioners, the authors should be careful to go through and make sure all power systems terminology and assumptions are properly defined, e.g.,:
* Line 92: Define "power curtailment"
* Line 127--129: It would be important to clarify why and when the distribution network can be modeled as a tree graph (especially since the 33-bus system evaluated is actually not a tree, though the authors mention in the appendix that they modify it to remove loops).
* Line 140: Define "per unit"

Other questions and comments:
* Lines 41--42: Voltage and power are indeed related, through the power flow equations, which means that the constraint and control action are indeed linked. Would it be possible to clarify what is meant by this statement?
* Line 140: "When the load is heavy during the nighttime" -- Why is load heavier at night, especially during seasons with no HVAC? Or is it just meant that *net* load is heavy due to low availability of PV power at night?
* Line 184--186: The current notation for $\mathcal{R}$ implies that it is a set of sets, rather than a union of sets.
* Line 191: What is meant by "at the last step"?
* Line 205, "State Transition Probability Function": The notation here should be cleaned up, as $\delta$ outputs a state but $Pr(s_{t+1}|s_{t})$ outputs a probability, so it's not immediately clear to me how $\mathcal{T}$ is actually defined.
* Line 339--340: More details should be given on how and why MARL beats droop control methods.

Minor:
* Line 193: "radius" --> "radians"
* Lines 194 and 195: "measures" --> "measurements"
* Line 218: Since "loss" can have multiple meanings in this context, it may be worth clarify that "loss" is used in the ML context here.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes, the authors have adequately addressed these points.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.code_of_conduct: While performing my duties as a reviewer (including writing reviews and participating in discussions), I have and will continue to abide by the NeurIPS code of conduct.

Summary:
This paper proposes a new environment for multi-agent reinforcement learning that addresses the problem of active voltage control on electric power distribution networks. The authors formulate the voltage control problem as a Dec-POMDP, and implement a number of different MARL baselines to assess their performance on the environment. They also assess the effect of different voltage loss functions on this environment.

Main Review:
I am very much on the borderline regarding this paper. On the one hand, I think it addresses a very important problem, and that it would be extremely beneficial to both the ML/MARL and power systems communities to have this kind of distribution system control environment readily available. On the other hand, I think one of the key assumptions underpinning the current framework needs to be better justified, that there should be more details about the actual usability of the environment, and that some of the technical notations and explanations need to be cleaned up.

Key assumption: One of my main concerns is regarding the assumption made in the paper that multiple inverters are controlled by one company that has full information within its operational region. I do not believe this model is common in present distribution networks -- that is, where smart inverter control exists, it is generally the case that each inverter operates separately based on local information. The authors should provide much more context around this assumption (e.g., how does this grid operation mode relate to existing grid operation modes?), both to avoid inadvertently misleading the ML community about the state of current grid operations, and to better justify the technical assumptions on which the rest of the work is based.

Usage instructions: Since the proposed contribution is an environment with baselines, I was surprised to see no details relating to the practical usability of the environment. For instance, would this environment be integrated within existing MARL frameworks, and/or does it have a user interface that is similar? Is the plan to provide a quickstart guide to help users easily get started? These kinds of details are important to understanding whether this kind of environment would actually be able to be widely adopted, or whether ML researchers/practitioners would find it difficult to use in practice.

Terminology and explanations: As one of the goals of the paper is to make the voltage control setting accessible to ML practitioners, the authors should be careful to go through and make sure all power systems terminology and assumptions are properly defined, e.g.,:
* Line 92: Define "power curtailment"
* Line 127--129: It would be important to clarify why and when the distribution network can be modeled as a tree graph (especially since the 33-bus system evaluated is actually not a tree, though the authors mention in the appendix that they modify it to remove loops).
* Line 140: Define "per unit"

Other questions and comments:
* Lines 41--42: Voltage and power are indeed related, through the power flow equations, which means that the constraint and control action are indeed linked. Would it be possible to clarify what is meant by this statement?
* Line 140: "When the load is heavy during the nighttime" -- Why is load heavier at night, especially during seasons with no HVAC? Or is it just meant that *net* load is heavy due to low availability of PV power at night?
* Line 184--186: The current notation for $\mathcal{R}$ implies that it is a set of sets, rather than a union of sets.

Yes, the authors have adequately addressed these points.

reviews.text:
Summary:  
The manuscript introduces a novel multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL) environment tailored for active voltage regulation in electric power distribution systems. The authors cast the voltage control challenge as a Dec-POMDP, implement several MARL algorithms as baselines, and explore the impact of different voltage-related loss functions within this setting.

Detailed Feedback:  
I find myself on the fence about this submission. Its focus on voltage control in distribution networks is certainly timely and has the potential to serve both the ML/MARL community and power systems researchers by providing an accessible testbed. However, there are three main areas that require further work: (1) a critical modeling assumption needs stronger justification; (2) the paper should describe how users will actually leverage the proposed environment; and (3) several notational and explanatory aspects must be clarified.

1. Key Modeling Assumption  
The authors assume that a single operator manages multiple smart inverters within a distribution feeder and possesses complete system information. In practice, inverter control is usually decentralized, with each device acting on local measurements rather than under one central authority. The paper should situate this assumption more clearly: how does this operational mode compare to real-world distribution practices? Offering concrete examples or references to existing grid-management frameworks would prevent confusion among ML practitioners and better ground the subsequent technical development.

2. Practical Usability of the Environment  
Given that the main deliverable is an environment accompanied by baseline agents, readers will want to know how easy it is to adopt. Will this environment plug into established MARL libraries (e.g., PettingZoo, RLlib)? Is there a plan for a quickstart tutorial or API reference? Details on installation, dependencies, example scripts, or visualization tools would greatly enhance confidence that the environment can achieve widespread adoption by ML researchers and engineers.

3. Terminology and Explanations  
To make the domain accessible to a broad ML audience, all power-engineering terms and modeling choices should be precisely defined:
  • Line 92: Please provide a definition of “power curtailment.”  
  • Lines 127–129: Clarify under what conditions a distribution network can be treated as a tree. The paper notes that the 33-bus test case is not originally a tree, and that loops were removed in the appendix—but this process deserves a clearer explanation in the main text.  
  • Line 140: Introduce the “per unit” system when first mentioned.

Additional Queries and Clarifications:  
  • Lines 41–42: The manuscript states that voltage and power are linked via the power-flow equations, implying the action and constraint are connected. Can the authors elaborate on this phrasing?  
  • Line 140: The phrase “When the load is heavy during the nighttime” is puzzling. Is nighttime demand genuinely higher, or is the authors’ intent to say that net load rises when photovoltaic output is zero?  
  • Lines 184–186: The notation for 𝓡 suggests a set of sets rather than their union. Please confirm the intended formalism.  
  • Line 191: What precisely does “at the last step” refer to in the sequence of episodes or time steps?  
  • Lines 205–206: The transition model’s notation is inconsistent. δ returns a next state, whereas Pr(sₜ₊₁ | sₜ) is a probability. The authors should reconcile these expressions or redefine 𝓣 clearly.  
  • Lines 339–340: The claim that MARL outperforms conventional droop-control schemes is intriguing. More explanation of why and under what metrics this holds would strengthen the contribution.

Minor Corrections:  
  • Line 193: Replace “radius” with “radians.”  
  • Lines 194–195: Change “measures” to “measurements.”  
  • Line 218: Since “loss” can denote both physical power loss and ML training loss, please specify that here it refers to the ML objective.

Limitations and Societal Impact:  
The authors have sufficiently discussed potential limitations and societal implications.

Recommendation:  
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold

reviews.summary: This paper introduces a new open-source environment for training RL agents on controlling the voltage of a power distribution network. The problem is formulated as a multi-agent Dec-POMDP. Agents control PV inverters using only local measurements coming from sensors situated in the region they operate. Their task is to maintain the voltage within safety range. Several MARL algorithms are evaluated in three different power networks. The paper also investigates different reward functions.
reviews.main_review:
The main contribution of this work is the introduction of a new benchmark domain for testing MARL algorithms. This consists of a simulator of a power network. The environment is built using existent network topologies and real data. I definitely agree with the authors' view on the importance of having realistic scenarios where RL methods can be tested appropriately before using them in real-world applications. It is very unfortunate that the code is not provided in the supplementary material. It is hard to assess how useful this work will be for the community without being able to test the environment.

I miss some intuitive explanation of the dynamics of the problem. Some technical details are given in the Appendix but these are hard to follow for someone lacking in knowledge of the field. In particular, I am looking for details like, how much past information about the network state is useful for making decisions. How do the actions taken in the past affect the network in the long run. I.e. what's the horizon of the problem? All this could be illustrated using some sample trajectories coming from the standard operation of the network. To what extent do the changes in the policies of the agents in one region affect other regions? I.e how strongly coupled are the different regions in the network? This information could shed some light on why some MARL algorithms seem to perform better than others.

It is not clear whether an agent controls one or multiple PV inverters. Why are there multiple agents for each region? As far as I can see, all agents in a region receive the same information. If that’s the case, wouldn’t it be more effective to have all PV inverters within a region be controlled by a single policy? 

The environment is partially observable, yet according to Section 5: MARL Algorithm Settings (line 283-287) the policies are modeled with MLPs receiving only the last two observations as input. Using only the last two observations doesn’t seem sufficient to satisfy the Markov assumption, especially considering the intrinsic non-stationarity of the problem (power generation and consumption fluctuate during the day).

Minor details:

h is first defined as a function that maps nodes to the corresponding subset of measures for that node (line 195) and then also used as the history length (line 228).
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.

reviews.text:
Rewritten Review:

Summary:  
The authors present an open-source simulation environment designed for training reinforcement learning agents to regulate voltages in power distribution networks. They frame the task as a multi-agent Dec-POMDP, where each agent manages the output of photovoltaic inverters based solely on local sensor data in its assigned region. Their goal is to keep voltages within prescribed safety limits. To demonstrate the environment’s utility, they benchmark several multi-agent RL algorithms across three distinct network topologies and experiment with various reward formulations.

Detailed Feedback:

1. Novelty and Contribution  
   • The key value of this paper lies in offering a realistic benchmark for multi-agent RL in power systems—a domain where practical testing is crucial before any real-world deployment. By leveraging existing network structures and authentic operational data, the environment fills an important gap.  
   • However, the absence of publicly available code in the supplementary material is a significant drawback. Without access to the implementation, it’s difficult for readers and practitioners to verify results or build upon this work.

2. Clarity on System Dynamics  
   • The manuscript would benefit from a more accessible, intuitive overview of the network’s dynamic behavior. While the Appendix contains mathematical details, these can be challenging for those unfamiliar with power system modeling.  
   • For instance, how far back in time do past states influence current decisions? What is the effective decision horizon—i.e., how long do inverter actions persist in affecting network voltages? Providing example voltage trajectories under normal operation could illustrate these points.  
   • Additionally, it isn’t clear how interdependent the regions are. Quantifying or visualizing how a policy shift in one area propagates elsewhere would help explain why certain MARL methods outperform others.

3. Agent Granularity and Observability  
   • The paper does not specify whether a single agent is responsible for one inverter or multiple devices. Moreover, each region hosts several agents that appear to receive identical observations. If they all see the same local measurements, combining them into one controller per region might simplify the setup and potentially boost performance.  
   • The authors describe the environment as partially observable, yet in Section 5 they equip policies with MLPs that only take the two most recent observations as input. Limiting input to two time steps seems unlikely to fulfill the Markov property, particularly given the day–night fluctuations in generation and load. A discussion on why this choice suffices—or experiments with longer observation windows—would strengthen the work.

4. Minor Issue  
   • The notation “h” is used inconsistently: first (line 195) as the measurement mapping from nodes, then later (line 228) to denote history length. Clarifying this would avoid confusion.

Overall Assessment and Rating:  
This paper contributes a much-needed realistic testbed for multi-agent RL in power distribution contexts, yet it falls short in clarity on some modeling choices and in providing the codebase for community use. These concerns notwithstanding, I find the work sufficiently novel and valuable for publication, meriting a rating of 6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold.

reviews.summary: The main contribution of the article is mention in the abstract "... establishes an open-source environment. It aims to bridge the gap between the power community and the MARL community". and In section 1: "There is no commonly accepted benchmark to provide the basis for fair comparison of different solutions. To facilitate further research on this topic, and to bridge the gap between the power community and MARL community", The authors seem to forget about L2RPN.
reviews.main_review:
- The authors say in section 1 "we present a comprehensive test-bench and open-source environment for MARL based active voltage control". The effort for standardization is not good enough because they do not seem to know what measures should be taken to this. If there has to be a standard framework for RL based learning of smart grids then it must include the following specifications
    - Definition of actions and their domains
    - Definition of states and effect of grid-users on the states
    - Definition of environment and its standard response to actions
    - Define standards for randomness and noise in states, observations, actions, and their effects. 
- The algorithms ànd networks need to be fully customizable. 


- Formulation is sketchy. The actions are only dependent on the reactive power, while other researchers have used many different types of actions. Restricting the algorithm to limited actions will definitely affect the results, but the authors do not seem to realize that. In section 5.3 they describe the difference of results between their algorithms and traditional solutions, but they seem to forget that they are not using the complete range of actions here. 

- The authors seem to forget that the network layers matter a lot with respect to the algorithm, see (https://openreview.net/forum?id=LmUJqB1Cz8)

- The authors also seem to have forgotten the effect of replay selection on the results. I conclude this by their choice reward function which they name as bowl function, which could also be a parabolic function too. Probably this is why they have doubts about their statements e.g. line 302-303, and section 5.3.

- The implementation details mentioned in the appendix do not add much to the article.

reviews.text:
Summary:  
The paper’s principal claim, as stated in the abstract (“…establishes an open-source environment. It aims to bridge the gap between the power community and the MARL community”) and reiterated in Section 1 (“There is no commonly accepted benchmark … To facilitate further research on this topic, and to bridge the gap between the power community and MARL community”), overlooks the existing L2RPN challenge, which already addresses many of these goals.

Detailed Review:  
1. Standardization Effort Falls Short  
   • The authors assert in Section 1 that they “present a comprehensive test-bench and open-source environment for MARL-based active voltage control,” yet do not specify the core elements required for a standard framework. A truly standardized RL platform for smart‐grid control should clearly define:  
     – The set of allowable actions and their domains  
     – The state space and how grid users influence those states  
     – The environment dynamics and its deterministic or stochastic responses to actions  
     – Protocols for injecting randomness/noise into states, observations, actions, and their effects  
   • In addition, the underlying algorithms and neural‐network architectures must be fully parameterizable to allow fair comparison across methods.

2. Overly Simplistic Formulation  
   • The paper’s action space is restricted exclusively to reactive‐power adjustments, whereas prior works incorporate a broader variety of control interventions. By limiting the action set so narrowly, the reported performance differences (Section 5.3) against conventional approaches are confounded by this incomplete action repertoire—an issue the authors do not acknowledge.

3. Neglect of Network Architecture Effects  
   • The manuscript fails to discuss how the choice and depth of neural‐network layers influence learning outcomes. For evidence of the importance of architecture on MARL results, see https://openreview.net/forum?id=LmUJqB1Cz8.

4. Replay‐Selection and Reward Design Overlooked  
   • The authors introduce a “bowl function” reward but do not consider how different replay‐buffer sampling strategies could alter learning dynamics. Given that this function closely resembles a parabolic form, it likely contributes to the authors’ uncertainty in interpreting their own results (cf. lines 302–303 and Section 5.3).

5. Limited Value of Appendix Implementation Details  
   • While the appendix enumerates implementation specifics, these details do not materially enhance the reader’s understanding of the method or its reproducibility beyond what is already clear from the main text.

Limitations and Societal Impact:  
The manuscript would benefit from a more thorough discussion of the proposed framework’s own limitations, as well as explicit acknowledgment of constraints inherent in the solution when deployed in realistic grid settings.

Rating: 5 – Marginally below the acceptance threshold
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The main contribution of the article is mention in the abstract "... establishes an open-source environment. It aims to bridge the gap between the power community and the MARL community". and In section 1: "There is no commonly accepted benchmark to provide the basis for fair comparison of different solutions. To facilitate further research on this topic, and to bridge the gap between the power community and MARL community", The authors seem to forget about L2RPN.

Main Review:
- The authors say in section 1 "we present a comprehensive test-bench and open-source environment for MARL based active voltage control". The effort for standardization is not good enough because they do not seem to know what measures should be taken to this. If there has to be a standard framework for RL based learning of smart grids then it must include the following specifications
    - Definition of actions and their domains
    - Definition of states and effect of grid-users on the states
    - Definition of environment and its standard response to actions
    - Define standards for randomness and noise in states, observations, actions, and their effects. 
- The algorithms ànd networks need to be fully customizable. 


- Formulation is sketchy. The actions are only dependent on the reactive power, while other researchers have used many different types of actions. Restricting the algorithm to limited actions will definitely affect the results, but the authors do not seem to realize that. In section 5.3 they describe the difference of results between their algorithms and traditional solutions, but they seem to forget that they are not using the complete range of actions here. 

- The authors seem to forget that the network layers matter a lot with respect to the algorithm, see (https://openreview.net/forum?id=LmUJqB1Cz8)

- The authors also seem to have forgotten the effect of replay selection on the results. I conclude this by their choice reward function which they name as bowl function, which could also be a parabolic function too. Probably this is why they have doubts about their statements e.g. line 302-303, and section 5.3.

- The implementation details mentioned in the appendix do not add much to the article.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
They need to be more elaborate about the limitations of the proposed technique/framework, secondly they should clearly mention the limitations of their solution within the proposed framework.

5: Marginally below the acceptance threshold

title: Differential Privacy of Dirichlet Posterior Sampling

reviews.summary: This paper proposes a method for releasing differentially private samples from Dirichlet posterior distribution. Authors present a privacy analysis for their proposed method and demonstrate that the method out performs Gaussian mechanism under certain conditions. Besides the empirical evaluation, authors also derive analytical utility guarantees for the method.
reviews.main_review:
In this paper authors propose a method that draws a single sample from Dirichlet posterior distribution. The method is rather simple and draws from Dir$(x + \alpha)$, which is the posterior of multinomial observations $x$ given a Dirichlet prior Dir$(\alpha)$ for the multinomial probabilities. The privacy of the method is analysed with respect to the prior parameter $\alpha$ using truncanted CDP. Authors consider two use cases for this method (which I think are rather similar), and derive analytical expressions for the utility of these cases. Finally authors demonstrate that the proposed method outperforms Gaussian method in a histogram release task when the number of samples is small.

The analysis of the paper seems novel to me. I appreciate the authors' efforts for deriving analytical expressions between the privacy and utility. I can also see a use case for such method (for example the histogram release demonstrated in Sec 4.2), however I think the use cases might be rather limited. Thus I think the method might not be the most striking from usage perspective, but with the analysis it is a solid privacy paper.

Pros:
- (+++) Very clearly written paper
- (++) Novel privacy analysis in terms of Dirichlet prior parameter $\alpha$
- (++) Utility analysis in terms of sample size
- (+) Emprical results showing improvement over Gaussian mech.

Cons:
- (--) Use case might be limited
- (--) I'm not sure what is the actual difference between uses in 4.1 and 4.2

Minor comments/questions:
- Introduction, lines 30-32. Are you talking about drawing from posterior predictive? In that case I think Y should also be drawn for each sample.
- Theorem 1, proof. Is there really $\alpha'$ separate from $\alpha$ or is it a typo?
- Figure 2, I guess it would make more sense to call the y-axis $\ell_2$-error instead of $\ell_2$-accuracy. For me it seems odd to say that "We see that the $\ell_2$-accuracy of the Dirichlet mechanism is smaller than that of the Gaussian mechanism for small N when d = 1000.", when you actually want to point out that your method works better in the small N domain, i.e. it's more "accurate".
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Yes

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Summary:
This manuscript introduces a technique for generating differentially private draws from a Dirichlet posterior. Specifically, it samples once from Dirichlet(x + α), where x are the observed multinomial counts and α is the Dirichlet prior parameter. The authors analyze privacy under truncated concentrated differential privacy (CDP) relative to α, derive closed-form utility bounds, and empirically compare their approach to the Gaussian mechanism. Their experiments show that, for small sample sizes, the Dirichlet-based method yields lower error in histogram release than the Gaussian alternative.

Detailed Review:
The core contribution is quite straightforward—take a single draw from Dir(x + α)—yet the authors back it with a careful privacy proof and utility analysis. They frame two closely related applications (Sections 4.1 and 4.2) and obtain analytic expressions linking sample size, prior parameter, and utility. Finally, they run experiments on histogram release tasks, demonstrating that under limited N and high dimension d, their method outperforms the standard Gaussian mechanism in ℓ₂-error.

I find the privacy analysis novel and appreciate the derivation of explicit privacy‐utility trade-offs. The histogram release example in Section 4.2 illustrates a practical scenario, though overall the target applications seem somewhat narrow. The presentation is very clear, and the theoretical results are sound. While the method itself is simple, the analytical treatment makes this a solid contribution to the differential privacy literature.

Pros:
- (+++) Exceptionally well written and easy to follow.
- (++) Innovative privacy analysis phrased in terms of the Dirichlet prior α.
- (++) Rigorous utility bounds as a function of sample size.
- (+) Empirical validation showing superior performance over the Gaussian mechanism in small-N settings.

Cons:
- (--) The scope of potential applications appears limited.
- (--) It is unclear how the two use cases in Sections 4.1 and 4.2 truly differ.

Minor Comments and Questions:
- Introduction (lines 30–32): Are you referring to sampling from the posterior predictive? If so, shouldn’t Y be drawn for each data point?
- Theorem 1 proof: Is the notation α′ distinct from α, or is that a typo?
- Figure 2: It may be clearer to label the vertical axis “ℓ₂-error” instead of “ℓ₂-accuracy.” Saying that the “ℓ₂-accuracy of the Dirichlet mechanism is smaller than that of the Gaussian mechanism” reads oddly when you intend to convey better performance (i.e., lower error) for small N.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
Yes

reviews.summary: Summary: This paper gives a precise analysis of the privacy loss, as measured by differential privacy, from drawing samples from the Dirichlet posterior distribution on multinomial parameters conditioned on a sensitive data set.
reviews.main_review:
The paper has a single main result, namely a precise analysis of the distribution of the privacy loss—in the sense of differential privacy—of the algorithm which takes a sensitive data set as input and a nonsensitive Dirichlet prior on parameters of a multinomial distribution, constructs a posterior distribution on the parameters, and samples a single value from that distribution. 

The main result (Theorem 1) is stated in terms of truncated concentrated DP, though I think it might be easier to state it in terms of Renyi DP (as a function of the $\alpha$ vector and the “order” $\lambda$) and then derive the tCDP and $(\epsilon,\delta)$-DP statements as corollaries. (I also think it would be worthwhile, in the corollaries, setting $\Delta_\infty$ and $\Delta_2$ to particular values, say 1, to get clear statements.) 

I have very few comments on the writing: the paper is clear and easy to understand and, I think, accurately represents and discusses the contributions. The only question here is significance: the paper is providing updated calculations for a well-know idea. It definitely should be published in an archival and refereed venue. I’m less sure that it meets the bar for NeurIPS. If the committee ultimately decides not to accept the paper, I would encourage the authors to submit to an appropriate journal.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: Well addressed in a separate section.

Rating:
6: Marginally above the acceptance threshold
reviews.text:
Summary:
This manuscript delivers a detailed, exact characterization of the differential privacy loss incurred when one samples a single draw from a Dirichlet posterior over multinomial parameters conditioned on a sensitive dataset.

Detailed Review:
The central accomplishment is a precise analysis (presented as Theorem 1) of the privacy-loss random variable for the mechanism that takes as input a confidential dataset and a non-sensitive Dirichlet prior over a multinomial distribution’s parameters, computes the posterior, and outputs one sampled parameter vector. 

In the current write-up, Theorem 1 is phrased using truncated concentrated DP (tCDP). It may be more transparent to restate this result in the language of Rényi DP—parameterized by the Dirichlet concentration vector α and the Rényi order λ—and then derive the tCDP and (ε, δ)-DP guarantees as immediate corollaries. In those corollaries, fixing Δ∞ and Δ₂ to concrete values (for instance, 1) would yield especially clear, readily interpretable bounds.

On exposition, the paper is very well written: the arguments are clear, the notation consistent, and the contribution accurately highlighted. My only hesitation concerns its level of novelty and impact: although the analysis is tight and useful, it refines an already familiar approach rather than introducing a fundamentally new technique. The work certainly belongs in an archival, peer-reviewed venue, but it may sit at the borderline of NeurIPS’s novelty bar. If the program committee opts against acceptance, I would nonetheless urge the authors to submit this to an appropriate journal.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
These aspects are thoughtfully addressed in their dedicated section.

reviews.summary: The paper studies releasing a single sample from a Dirichlet posterior with several variants of differential privacy in the two specific tasks of Dirichlet sampling and private normalized histogram publishing. Both privacy and utility guarantees are analyzed by the authors. The authors compare the results with Gaussian mechanism theoretically and empirically using a simple simulation.
reviews.main_review: The paper is well-organized and easy to follow. The related works are summarized and allow the reader to compare the drawbacks of existing methods relatively to the proposed methods. All the notations and background definitions are clearly stated. While the paper contains a significant amount of theoretical derivations, many of them are accompanied by intuitions and interpretations (such as discussion of low/high privacy regimes or illustrations of  behavior of the computed quantities as a function of parameters, such as Fig. 1). I was not able to thoroughly verify all the derivations, but the math seems sounds. Overall, i believe the paper makes a good contribution and will be interesting to the researchers in the area.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact: The paper contains a section on "Potential negative societal impacts" which reasonably discussed the implications of the usage of the method in an industrial setting.

reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper studies releasing a single sample from a Dirichlet posterior with several variants of differential privacy in the two specific tasks of Dirichlet sampling and private normalized histogram publishing. Both privacy and utility guarantees are analyzed by the authors. The authors compare the results with Gaussian mechanism theoretically and empirically using a simple simulation.

Main Review:
The paper is well-organized and easy to follow. The related works are summarized and allow the reader to compare the drawbacks of existing methods relatively to the proposed methods. All the notations and background definitions are clearly stated. While the paper contains a significant amount of theoretical derivations, many of them are accompanied by intuitions and interpretations (such as discussion of low/high privacy regimes or illustrations of  behavior of the computed quantities as a function of parameters, such as Fig. 1). I was not able to thoroughly verify all the derivations, but the math seems sounds. Overall, i believe the paper makes a good contribution and will be interesting to the researchers in the area.

Limitations And Societal Impact:
The paper contains a section on "Potential negative societal impacts" which reasonably discussed the implications of the usage of the method in an industrial setting.

Rating:
7: Good paper, accept
reviews.text:
Summary:
This manuscript investigates the problem of releasing a single draw from a Dirichlet posterior under various differential privacy frameworks, focusing on two settings: sampling from a Dirichlet distribution and publishing a privately normalized histogram. The authors derive both privacy and utility bounds for their mechanisms and benchmark their approach against the Gaussian mechanism through theoretical analysis and straightforward simulations.

Detailed Review:
The paper is clearly structured and straightforward to read. The related work section effectively outlines existing approaches and highlights their limitations in comparison to the proposed methods. Notation and background material are presented unambiguously. Although the paper is dense with theoretical developments, the authors consistently provide intuition and explanations—such as discussions contrasting low- and high-privacy regimes and graphical depictions of how key quantities vary with parameters (e.g., Figure 1). While I did not verify every step of the proofs in detail, the mathematical arguments appear correct. On balance, this work offers a meaningful advance and should attract interest from researchers working on private Bayesian inference and histogram release.

Limitations and Societal Impact:
The authors include a section on “Potential negative societal impacts,” in which they thoughtfully consider how their methods might be used or misused in real-world, industrial scenarios.

Recommendation:
7: Good paper, accept

reviews.summary: This paper studies differential privacy and the dirichlet mechanism.
reviews.main_review:
The paper contains quite some phrases which aren't fully precise.  Often, a minor effort can make the text more precise without taking significantly more space.  In some other cases, the question is whether it is worthwhile to give technical details without giving the reader the context in which he can understand these details.  E.g.,
* Line 38: "DP allows us to quantify how much the privacy of the Dirichlet posterior sampling is affected by the prior parameters" -> what is meant here with "afect"?  Maybe you want to analyze how privacy guarantees depend on these parameters, but the cause of the change in privacy is in the publishing of the output so I wouldn't use "affect" for the parameters.
* Line 67: "any posterior sampling with the log-likelihood bounded by B is 4B-differentially private" -> which log-likelihood must be bounded by B ?  Do you mean classic \epsilon-differential privacy here (where you use B instead of \epsilon for some other reason) ?  Usually a loglikelihood B is a negative value, while \epsilon=4B must be positive to be able to consider \epsilon-DP.  Should the loglikelihood be upper bounded or lower bounded by B ?
* line 70: "if the condition on the log-likelihood is relaxed to the Lipschitz continuity with high probability," -> the uninformed reader reading only your summary may not understand how a log-likelihood bound |log(P(x))| < B (which is typically applied to random variables) can be relaxed to a Lipschitz continuity requirement (which is typically applied to functions).  
* Line 74: "the sufficient statistics x" refers to some not-yet-introduced variables x on which "the" sufficient statistics is considered without explaining to the reader which sufficient statistics is being considered.
* Line 165: The theorem uses expressions involving x and x'.  x and x' are sample statistics of datasets, but it is unclear how they are sampled (e.g., with replacement / without replacement or other sample properties).  Even though we learn that x and x' are sample statistics from two adjacent datasets.
* Theorem 1, and also a few earlier sentences, use the term "one-time sampling", but this concept isn't defined (and also not a hit when provided to Google).  Do you mean "a single value x drawn from the distribution" (a single sample) or do you mean "at one time we draw many values x_1, x_2, x_3 ... without doing other things in between" (e.g., in contrast to MDP no decision can be made after a first sample to infleunce a later sample), or something else?

There are some minor language issue, often related to the use of articles where I wouldn't expect them or the absence of articles where I would expect them.  Also,
* Line 128: "... privacy" -> ... private

Some other comments:
* Line 142 saying "p(x|y)" suggests a conditional probability distribution where y is known and the distribution of drawing next x depends on y.  On the other hand, the equation between lines 144 and 145 suggests that we need to know x in order to draw Y.
* Line 150 discusses "Consider a special case where x = p", but p isn't introduced yet at that point: the only thing we know about p already is (from the notations section) that \sum_i p_i = 1.
* It turns out that p is an empirical distribution of a dataset.  The paragraph mentions the question how to make a private version of p (a problem which has been solved without using Dirichlet distributions), but doesn't immediately provide an answer.  
* Figure 2: I guess that instead of "accuracy" (typically used in classification problems as the fraction of cases classified correctly, so ideally close to 1) you mean "norm" or "loss" (ideally small), at least this is what the Y-axis scale suggests.

The paper doesn't clearly define the problem it is trying to solve.  E.g., What values are sensitive and need to be protected from being revealed?  How does the "one-time sampling" differ from earlier work which considered the same problem but without "one-time sampling" ?


In conclusion, the paper has numerous minor issues (which individually can probably be easily solved but collectively may form a serious hurdle for a non-expert reader), introduces insufficiently clearly the problem (a problem formulation is the key part of a paper which should be understandable for all Neurips attendees), clarifies insufficiently in which way the contribution is novel and significant, and only in a limited way validates the work through experiments (with a single Gaussian baseline) where it would be possible to compare to other alternatives and discuss limitations and advantages.
reviews.limitations_and_societal_impact:
The text presents a comparison between the Guassian and Dirichlet mechanisms, and highlights around line 280 that the accuracy of the Dirichlet mechanism is better for spartse histograms and small N, but is silent about limitations, e.g., in the cases of dense hisograms and large N.

The paper doesn't discuss limitations, nor plans for future work where such limitations could be addressed.
